william makepeace thackeray the history of pendennis chapter i show how first love may interrupt breakfast one fine morning in the full london season major arthur pendennis come over from his lodging accord to his custom to breakfast at a certain club in pall mall of which he be a chief ornament
as he be one of the fine judge of wine in england and a man of active dominating and inquire spirit he have be very properly choose to be a member of the committee of this club and indeed be almost the manager of the institution and the steward and waiter bow before he as reverentially as to a duke or a field-marshal
at a quarter past ten the major invariably make his appearance in the good black boot in all london with a check morning cravat that never be rumple until dinner time a buff waistcoat which bear the crown of his sovereign on the button and linen so spotless that mr
brummel himself ask the name of his laundress and would probably have employ she have not misfortune compel that great man to fly the country
pendennis s coat his white glove his whisker his very cane be perfect of their kind as specimen of the costume of a military man en retraite
at a distance or see his back merely you would have take he to be not more than thirty year old it be only by a nearer inspection that you see the factitious nature of his rich brown hair and that there be a few crow foot round about the somewhat fade eye of his handsome mottled face
his nose be of the wellington pattern
his hand and wristband be beautifully long and white
on the latter he wear handsome gold button give to he by his royal highness the duke of york and on the other more than one elegant ring the chief and large of they be emblazon with the famous arm of pendennis
he always take possession of the same table in the same corner of the room from which nobody ever now think of oust he
one or two mad wag and wild fellow have in former day and in freak or bravado endeavour twice or thrice to deprive he of this place but there be a quiet dignity in the major s manner as he take his seat at the next table and survey the interloper which render it impossible for any man to sit and breakfast under his eye and that table--by the fire and yet near the window--become his own
his letter be lay out there in expectation of his arrival and many be the young fellow about town who look with wonder at the number of those note and at the seal and frank which they bore
if there be any question about etiquette society who be married to whom of what age such and such a duke be pendennis be the man to whom every one appeal
marchioness use to drive up to the club and leave note for he or fetch he out
he be perfectly affable
the young man like to walk with he in the park or down pall mall for he touch his hat to everybody and every other man he meet be a lord
the major sate down at his accustomed table then and while the waiter go to bring he his toast and his hot newspaper he survey his letter through his gold double eye-glass
he carry it so gaily you would hardly have know it be spectacle in disguise and examine one pretty note after another and lay they by in order
there be large solemn dinner card suggestive of three course and heavy conversation there be neat little confidential note convey female entreaty there be a note on thick official paper from the marquis of steyne tell he to come to richmond to a little party at the star and garter and speak french which language the major possess very perfectly and another from the bishop of ealing and mrs
trail request the honour of major pendennis s company at ealing house all of which letter pendennis read gracefully and with the more satisfaction because glowry the scotch surgeon breakfast opposite to he be look on and hat he for have so many invitation which nobody ever send to glowry
these peruse the major take out his pocket-book to see on what day he be disengage and which of these many hospitable call he could afford to accept or decline
he throw over cutler the east india director in baker street in order to dine with lord steyne and the little french party at the star and garter--the bishop he accept because though the dinner be slow he like to dine with bishop--and so go through his list and dispose of they accord to his fancy or interest
then he take his breakfast and look over the paper the gazette the birth and death and the fashionable intelligence to see that his name be down among the guest at my lord so-and-sobe fete and in the interval of these occupation carry on cheerful conversation with his acquaintance about the room
among the letter which form major pendennis s budget for that morning there be only one unread and which lie solitary and apart from all the fashionable london letter with a country postmark and a homely seal
the superscription be in a pretty delicate female hand and though mark immediate by the fair writer with a strong dash of anxiety under the word yet the major have for reason of his own neglect up to the present moment his humble rural petitioner who to be sure could hardly hope to get a hearing among so many grand folk who attend his levee
the fact be this be a letter from a female relative of pendennis and while the grandee of her brother s acquaintance be receive and get their interview and drive off as it be the patient country letter remain for a long time wait for an audience in the ante-chamber under the slop-bason
at last it come to be this letter s turn and the major break a seal with fairoaks engrave upon it and clavering st
mary s for a postmark
it be a double letter and the major commence peruse the envelope before he attack the inner epistle
be it a letter from another jook growl mr
glowry inwardly pendennis would not be leave that to the last ibe think my dear major pendennis the letter run i beg and implore you to come to i immediately very likely think pendennis and steyne s dinner to-day-- i be in the very great grief and perplexity
my dear boy who have be hitherto everything the fond mother could wish be grieve i dreadfully
he have form--i can hardly write it--a passion an infatuation the major grinned-- for an actress who have be perform here
she be at least twelve year old than arthur--who will not be eighteen till next february--and the wretched boy insist upon marry she hay
whatbe make pendennis swear now mr
glowry ask of himself for rage and wonder be concentrate in the major s open mouth as he read this astounding announcement
do my dear friend the grief-stricken lady go on come to i instantly on the receipt of this and as arthur s guardian entreat command the wretched child to give up this most deplorable resolution and after more entreaty to the above effect the writer conclude by sign herself the major s unhappy affectionate sister helen pendennis fairoaks tuesday the major conclude read the last word of the letter-- a d---d pretty business at fairoaks tuesday now let we see what the boy have to say and he take the other letter which be write in a great floundering boy s hand and seal with the large signet of the pendennises even large than the major s own and with supplementary wax sputter all round the seal in token of the writer s tremulousness and agitation
the epistle run thus fairoaks monday midnight
my dear uncle in inform you of my engagement with miss costigan daughter of j
chesterfield costigan esq of costiganstown but perhaps well know to you under her professional name of miss fotheringay of the theatres royal drury lane and crow street and of the norwich and welsh circuit i be aware that i make an announcement which cannot accord to the present prejudice of society at least be welcome to my family
my dear mother on whom god know i would wish to inflict no needless pain be deeply move and grieve i be sorry to say by the intelligence which i have this night convey to she
i beseech you my dear sir to come down and reason with she and console she
although oblige by poverty to earn an honourable maintenance by the exercise of her splendid talent miss costigan s family be as ancient and noble as our own
when our ancestor ralph pendennis land with richard ii
in ireland my emily s forefather be king of that country
i have the information from mr
costigan who like yourself be a military man
it be in vain i have attempt to argue with my dear mother and prove to she that a young lady of irreproachable character and lineage endow with the most splendid gift of beauty and genius who devote herself to the exercise of one of the noble profession for the sacred purpose of maintain her family be a be whom we should all love and reverence rather than avoid my poor mother have prejudice which it be impossible for my logic to overcome and refuse to welcome to her arm one who be disposed to be her most affectionate daughter through life
although miss costigan be some year old than myself that circumstance do not operate as a barrier to my affection and i be sure will not influence its duration
a love like mine sir i feel be contract once and for ever
as i never have dream of love until i see she--i feel now that i shall die without ever know another passion
it be the fate of my life
it be miss c s own delicacy which suggest that the difference of age which i never feel might operate as a bar to our union
but have love once i should despise myself and be unworthy of my name as a gentleman if i hesitate to abide by my passion if i do not give all where i feel all and endow the woman who love i fondly with my whole heart and my whole fortune
i press for a speedy marriage with my emily--for why in truth should it be delay
a delay imply a doubt which i cast from i as unworthy
it be impossible that my sentiment can change towards emily--that at any age she can be anything but the sole object of my love
why then wait
i entreat you my dear uncle to come down and reconcile my dear mother to our union and i address you as a man of the world qui more hominum multorum vidit et urbe who will not feel any of the weak scruple and fear which agitate a lady who have scarcely ever leave her village
pray come down to we immediately
i be quite confident that--apart from consideration of fortune--you will admire and approve of my emily your affectionate nephew arthur pendennis jr when the major have conclude the perusal of this letter his countenance assume an expression of such rage and horror that glowry the surgeon-official feel in his pocket for his lancet which he always carry in his card-case and think his respected friend be go into a fit
the intelligence be indeed sufficient to agitate pendennis
the head of the pendennises go to marry an actress ten year his senior a headstrong boy go to plunge into matrimony
the mother have spoil the young rascal groan the major inwardly with her cursed sentimentality and romantic rubbish
my nephew marry a tragedy queen
gracious mercy people will laugh at i so that i shall not dare show my head and he think with an inexpressible pang that he must give up lord steyne s dinner at richmond and must lose his rest and pass the night in an abominable tight mail-coach instead of take pleasure as he have promise himself in some of the most agreeable and select society in england
and he must not only give up this but all other engagement for some time to come
who know how long the business might detain he
he quit his breakfast table for the adjoining writing-room and there ruefully write off refusal to the marquis the earl the bishop and all his entertainer and he order his servant to take place in the mail-coach for that evening of course charge the sum which he disburse for the seat to the account of the widow and the young scapegrace of whom he be guardian
chapter ii a pedigree and other family matter early in the regency of george the magnificent there live in a small town in the west of england call clavering a gentleman whose name be pendennis
there be those alive who remember have see his name paint on a board which be surmount by a gilt pestle and mortar over the door of a very humble little shop in the city of bath where mr
pendennis exercise the profession of apothecary and surgeon and where he not only attend gentleman in their sick-room and lady at the most interesting period of their life but would condescend to sell a brown-paper plaster to a farmer s wife across the counter or to vend tooth-brush hair-powder and london perfumery
for these fact a few folk at clavering could vouch where people s memory be more tenacious perhaps than they be in a great bustling metropolis
and yet that little apothecary who sell a stray customer a pennyworth of salt or a more fragrant cake of windsor soap be a gentleman of good education and of as old a family as any in the whole county of somerset
he have a cornish pedigree which carry the pendennises up to the time of the druids and who know how much far back
they have intermarry with the normans at a very late period of their family existence and they be relate to all the great family of wales and brittany
pendennis have have a piece of university education too and might have pursue that career with great honour but that in his second year at cambridge his father die insolvent and poor pen be oblige to betake himself to the pestle and apron
he always detest the trade and it be only necessity and the offer of his mother s brother a london apothecary of low family into which pendennis s father have demean himself by marry that force john pendennis into so odious a calling
he quickly after his apprenticeship part from the coarse-minded practitioner his relative and set up for himself at bath with his modest medical ensign
he have for some time a hard struggle with poverty and it be all he could do to keep the shop and its gilt ornament in decent repair and his bed-ride mother in comfort but lady ribstone happen to be pass to the rooms with an intoxicated irish chairman who bump her ladyship up against pen s very door-post and drive his chair-pole through the handsome pink bottle in the surgeon s window alight scream from her vehicle and be accommodate with a chair in mr
pendennis s shop where she be bring round with cinnamon and sal-volatile
mr
pendennis s manner be so uncommonly gentlemanlike and soothing that her ladyship the wife of sir pepin ribstone of codlingbury in the county of somerset bart appoint her preserver as she call he apothecary to her person and family which be very large
master ribstone come home for the christmas holiday from eton over-eat himself and have a fever in which mr
pendennis treat he with the great skill and tenderness
in a word he get the good grace of the codlingbury family and from that day begin to prosper
the good company of bath patronise he and amongst the lady especially he be beloved and admire
first his humble little shop become a smart one then he discard the selling of tooth-brush and perfumery as unworthy of a gentleman of an ancient lineage then he shut up the shop altogether and only have a little surgery attend by a genteel young man then he have a gig with a man to drive he and before her exit from this world his poor old mother have the happiness of see from her bedroom window to which her chair be roll her beloved john step into a close carriage of his own a one-horse carriage it be true but with the arm of the family of pendennis handsomely emblazon on the panel
what would arthur say now she ask speak of a young son of hers-- who never so much as once come to see my dearest johnny through all the time of his poverty and struggle captain pendennis be with his regiment in india mother mr
pendennis remark and if you please i wish you would not call i johnny before the young man--before mr
parkins presently the day come when she cease to call her son by the name of johnny or by any other title of endearment or affection and his house be very lonely without that kind though querulous voice
he have his night-bell alter and place in the room in which the good old lady have grumble for many a long year and he sleep in the great large bed there
he be upwards of forty year old when these event befell before the war be over before george the magnificent come to the throne before this history indeed but what be a gentleman without his pedigree
pendennis by this time have his handsomely frame and glaze and hang up in his drawing-room between the picture of codlingbury house in somersetshire and st
boniface s college cambridge where he have pass the brief and happy day of his early manhood
as for the pedigree he have take it out of a trunk as sterne s officer call for his sword now that he be a gentleman and could show it
about the time of mrs
pendennis s demise another of her son s patient likewise die at bath that virtuous woman old lady pontypool daughter of reginald twelfth earl of bareacres and by consequence great-grand-aunt to the present earl and widow of john second lord pontypool and likewise of the reverend jonas wales of the armageddon chapel clifton
for the last five year of her life her ladyship have be attend by miss helen thistlewood a very distant relative of the noble house of bareacres before mention and daughter of lieutenant r
thistlewood r n kill at the battle of copenhagen
under lady pontypool s roof miss thistlewood find a comfortable shelter as far as boarding and lodging go but suffer under such an infernal tyranny as only woman can inflict on or bear from one another the doctor who pay his visit to my lady pontypool at least twice a day could not but remark the angelical sweetness and kindness with which the young lady bear her elderly relative s insult and it be as they be go in the fourth mourning coach to attend her ladyship s venerate remain to bath abbey where they now repose that he look at her sweet pale face and resolve upon put a certain question to she the very nature of which make his pulse beat ninety at least
he be old than she by more than twenty year and at no time the most ardent of man
perhaps he have have a love affair in early life which he have to strangle--perhaps all early love affair ought to be strangle or drown like so many blind kitten well at three-and-forty he be a collect quiet little gentleman in black stocking with a bald head and a few day after the ceremony he call to see she and as he feel her pulse he keep hold of her hand in his and ask she where she be go to live now that the pontypool family have come down upon the property which be be nail into box and pack into hamper and swaddle up with hayband and bury in straw and lock under three key in green baize plate-chest and cart away under the eye of poor miss helen he ask she where she be go to live finally
her eye fill with tear and she say she do not know
she have a little money
the old lady have leave she a thousand pound indeed and she would go into a boarding-house or into a school in fine she do not know where
then pendennis look into her pale face and keep hold of her cold little hand ask she if she would come and live with he
he be old compare to--to so bloom a young lady as miss thistlewood pendennis be of the grave old complimentary school of gentleman and apothecary but he be of good birth and he flatter himself of good principle and temper
his prospect be good and daily mending
he be alone in the world and have need of a kind and constant companion whom it would be the study of his life to make happy in a word he recite to she a little speech which he have compose that morning in bed and rehearse and perfect in his carriage as he be come to wait upon the young lady
perhaps if he have have an early love-passage she too have one day hop for a different lot than to be wed to a little gentleman who rap his tooth and smile artificially who be laboriously polite to the butler as he slide upstairs into the drawing-room and profusely civil to the lady s-maid who wait at the bed-room door for whom her old patroness use to ring as for a servant and who come with even more eagerness who get up story as he send in draught for his patient s amusement and his own profit perhaps she would have choose a different man--but she know on the other hand how worthy pendennis be how prudent how honourable how good he have be to his mother and constant in his care of she and the upshot of this interview be that she blush very much make pendennis an extremely low curtsey and ask leave to--to consider his very kind proposal
they be marry in the dull bath season which be the height of the season in london
and pendennis have previously through a professional friend m r c s secured lodging in holles street cavendish square take his wife thither in a chaise and pair conduct she to the theatre the parks and the chapel royal show she the folk go to a drawing-room and in a word give she all the pleasure of the town
he likewise leave card upon lord pontypool upon the right honourable the earl of bareacres and upon sir pepin and lady ribstone his early and kind patron
bareacre take no notice of the card
pontypool call admire mrs
pendennis and say lady pontypool would come and see she which her ladyship do per proxy of john her footman who bring her card and an invitation to a concert five week off
pendennis be back in his little one-horse carriage dispense draught and pill at that time but the ribstones ask he and mrs
pendennis to an entertainment of which mr
pendennis brag to the last day of his life
the secret ambition of mr
pendennis have always be to be a gentleman
it take much time and careful saving for a provincial doctor whose gain be not very large to lay by enough money wherewith to purchase a house and land but besides our friend s own frugality and prudence fortune aid he considerably in his endeavour and bring he to the point which he so panted to attain
he lay out some money very advantageously in the purchase of a house and small estate close upon the village of clavering before mention
word cannot describe nor do he himself ever care to confess to any one his pride when he find himself a real land proprietor and could walk over acre of which he be the master
a lucky purchase which he have make of share in a copper-mine add very considerably to his wealth and he realise with great prudence while this mine be still at its full vogue
finally he sell his business at bath to mr
parkins for a handsome sum of ready money and for an annuity to be pay to he during a certain number of year after he have for ever retire from the handling of the mortar and pestle
arthur pendennis his son be eight year old at the time of this event so that it be no wonder that the latter who leave bath and the surgery so young should forget the existence of such a place almost entirely and that his father s hand have ever be dirty by the compounding of odious pill or the preparation of filthy plaster
the old man never speak about the shop himself never allude to it call in the medical practitioner of clavering to attend his family when occasion arrive sink the black breech and stocking altogether attend market and session and wear a bottle-green coat and brass button with drab gaiter just as if he have be an english gentleman all his life
he use to stand at his lodge-gate and see the coach come in and bow gravely to the guard and coachman as they touch their hat and drive by
it be he who found the clavering book club and set up the samaritan soup and blanket society
it be he who bring the mail which use to run through cacklefield before away from that village and through clavering
at church he be equally active as a vestryman and a worshipper
at market every thursday he go from pen to stall look at sample of oat and munch corn feel beast punch geese in the breast and weigh they with a knowing air and do business with the farmer at the clavering arms as well as the old frequenter of that house of call
it be now his shame as it formerly be his pride to be call doctor and those who wish to please he always give he the title of squire
heaven know where they come from but a whole range of pendennis portrait presently hang round the doctor s oak dining-room lelys and vandykes he vow all the portrait to be and when question as to the history of the original would vaguely say they be ancestor of his you could see by his wife s look that she disbelieve in these genealogical legend for she generally endeavour to turn the conversation when he commence they
but his little boy believe they to their full extent and roger pendennis of agincourt arthur pendennis of crecy general pendennis of blenheim and oudenarde be as real and actual being for this young gentleman as--whom shall we say as robinson crusoe or peter wilkins or the seven champions of christendom whose history be in his library
pendennis s fortune which at the good be not above eight hundred pound a year do not with the good economy and management permit of his live with the great folk of the county but he have a decent comfortable society of the second-good sort
if they be not the rose they live near the rose as it be and have a good deal of the odour of genteel life
they have out their plate and din each other round in the moonlight night twice a year come a dozen mile to these festival and besides the county the pendennises have the society of the town of clavering as much as nay more than they like for mrs
pybus be always poke about helen s conservatory and intercept the operation of her soup-ticket and coal-club captain glanders h
p 50 th dragoon guards be for ever swagger about the squire s stable and garden and endeavour to enlist he in his quarrel with the vicar with the postmaster with the reverend f
wapshot of clavering grammar school for overflogge his son anglesea glanders with all the village in fine
and pendennis and his wife often bless themselves that their house of fairoaks be nearly a mile out of clavering or their premise would never have be free from the pry eye and prattle of one or other of the male and female inhabitant there
fairoak lawn come down to the little river brawl and on the other side be the plantation and wood as much as be leave of they of clavering park sir francis clavering bart
the park be let out in pasture and feed down by sheep and cattle when the pendennises come first to live at fairoaks
shutter be up in the house a splendid freestone palace with great stair statue and portico whereof you may see a picture in the beauties of england and wales sir richard clavering sir francis s-grandfather have commence the ruin of the family by the building of this palace his successor have achieve the ruin by live in it
the present sir francis be abroad somewhere nor could anybody be find rich enough to rent that enormous mansion through the deserted room mouldy clank hall and dismal gallery of which arthur pendennis many a time walk tremble when he be a boy
at sunset from the lawn of fairoaks there be a pretty sight it and the opposite park of clavering be in the habit of put on a rich golden tinge which become they both wonderfully
the upper window of the great house flame so as to make your eye wink the little river run off noisily westward and be lose in a sombre wood behind which the tower of the old abbey church of clavering whereby that town be call clavering st
mary s to the present day rise up in purple splendour
little arthur s figure and his mother s cast long blue shadow over the grass and he would repeat in a low voice for a scene of great natural beauty always move the boy who inherit this sensibility from his mother certain line begin these be thy glorious work parent of good almighty
thine this universal frame greatly to mrs
pendennis s delight
such walk and conversation generally end in a profusion of filial and maternal embrace for to love and to pray be the main occupation of this dear woman s life and i have often hear pendennis say in his wild way that he feel that he be sure of go to heaven for his mother never could be happy there without he
as for john pendennis as the father of the family and that sort of thing everybody have the great respect for he and his order be obey like those of the medes and persians
his hat be as well brush perhaps as that of any man in this empire
his meal be serve at the same minute every day and woe to those who come late as little pen a disorderly little rascal sometimes do
prayer be recite his letter be read his business dispatch his stable and garden inspect his hen-house and kennel his barn and pigstye visit always at regular hour
after dinner he always have a nap with the globe newspaper on his knee and his yellow bandanna handkerchief on his face major pendennis send the yellow handkerchief from india and his brother have help in the purchase of his majority so that they be good friend now
and so as his dinner take place at six o clock to a minute and the sunset business allude to may be suppose to have occur at about half-past seven it be probable that he do not much care for the view in front of his lawn window or take any share in the poetry and caress which be take place there
they seldom occur in his presence
however frisky they be before mother and child be hush and quiet when mr
pendennis walk into the drawing-room his newspaper under his arm
and here while little pen bury in a great chair read all the book of which he could lay hold the squire peruse his own article in the gardener s gazette or take a solemn hand at picquet with mrs
pendennis or an occasional friend from the village
pendennis usually take care that at least one of his grand dinner should take place when his brother the major who on the return of his regiment from india and new south wales have sell out and go upon half-pay come to pay his biennial visit to fairoaks
my brother major pendennis be a constant theme of the retired doctor s conversation
all the family delight in my brother the major
he be the link which bind they to the great world of london and the fashion
he always bring down the last news of the nobility and be in the constant habit of dining with lord and great folk
he speak of such with soldierlike respect and decorum
he would say my lord bareacres have be good enough to invite i to bareacres for the pheasant shooting or my lord steyne be so kind as to wish for my presence at stillbrook for the easter holiday and you may be sure the whereabouts of my brother the major be carefully make known by worthy mr
pendennis to his friend at the clavering reading room at justice-meeting or at the county-town
their carriage would come from ten mile round to call upon major pendennis in his visit to fairoaks the fame of his fashion as a man about town be establish throughout the county
there be a talk of his marry miss hunkle of lilybank old hunkle the attorney s daughter with at least fifteen hundred a-year to her fortune but my brother the major refuse this negotiation advantageous as it might seem to most person
as a bachelor he say nobody care how poor i be
i have the happiness to live with people who be so highly place in the world that a few hundred or thousand a year more or less can make no difference in the estimation in which they be pleased to hold i
miss hunkle though a most respectable lady be not in possession of either the birth or the manner which would entitle she to be receive into the sphere in which i have the honour to move
i shall live and die an old bachelor john and your worthy friend miss hunkle i have no doubt will find some more worthy object of her affection than a wear-out old soldier on half-pay time show the correctness of the surmise of the old man of the world miss hunkle marry a young french nobleman and be now at this moment live at lilybank under the title of baroness de carambole have be separate from her wild young scapegrace of a baron very shortly after their union
the major be a great favourite with almost all the little establishment of fairoaks
he be as good-natured as he be well breed and have a sincere liking and regard for his sister-in-law whom he pronounce and with perfect truth to be as fine a lady as any in england and an honour to the family
indeed mrs
pendennis s tranquil beauty her natural sweetness and kindness and that simplicity and dignity which a perfect purity and innocence be sure to bestow upon a handsome woman render she quite worthy of her brother s praise
i think it be not national prejudice which make i believe that a high-breed english lady be the most complete of all heaven s subject in this world
in whom else do you see so much grace and so much virtue so much faith and so much tenderness with such a perfect refinement and chastity
and by high-breed lady i donot mean duchess and countess
be they ever so high in station they can be but lady and no more
but almost every man who live in the world have the happiness let we hope of count a few such person amongst his circle of acquaintance--woman in whose angelical nature there be something awful as well as beautiful to contemplate at whose foot the wild and fiercest of we must fall down and humble ourselves in admiration of that adorable purity which never seem to do or to think wrong
arthur pendennis have the good fortune to have a mother endow with these happy quality
during his childhood and youth the boy think of she as little less than an angel as a supernatural being all wisdom love and beauty
when her husband drive she into the county town or to the assize ball or concert there he would step into the assembly with his wife on his arm and look the great folk in the face as much as to say look at that my lord can any of you show i a woman like that she enrage some country lady with three time her money by a sort of desperate perfection which they find in she
miss pybus say she be cold and haughty miss pierce that she be too proud for her station mrs
wapshot as a doctor of divinity s lady would have the pas of she who be only the wife of a medical practitioner
in the meanwhile this lady move through the world quite regardless of all the comment that be make in her praise or disfavour
she do not seem to know that she be admire or hat for be so perfect but carry on calmly through life say her prayer love her family help her neighbour and do her duty
that even a woman should be faultless however be an arrangement not permit by nature which assign to we mental defect as it award to us headache illness or death without which the scheme of the world could not be carry on nay some of the good quality of mankind could not be bring into exercise
as pain produce or elicit fortitude and endurance difficulty perseverance poverty industry and ingenuity danger courage and what not so the very virtue on the other hand will generate some vice and in fine mrs
pendennis have that vice which miss pybus and miss pierce discover in she namely that of pride which do not vest itself so much in her own person as in that of her family
she speak about mr
pendennis a worthy little gentleman enough but there be other as good as he with an awful reverence as if he have be the pope of rome on his throne and she a cardinal kneeling at his foot and give he incense
the major she hold to be a sort of bayard among majors and as for her son arthur she worship that youth with an ardour which the young scapegrace accept almost as coolly as the statue of the saint in saint peter s receive the rapturous osculation which the faithful deliver on his toe
this unfortunate superstition and idol-worship of this good woman be the cause of a great deal of the misfortune which befell the young gentleman who be the hero of this history and deserve therefore to be mention at the outset of his story
arthur pendennis s schoolfellow at the greyfriars school state that as a boy he be in no way remarkable either as a dunce or as a scholar
he do in fact just as much as be require of he and no more
if he be distinguish for anything it be for verse-writing but be his enthusiasm ever so great it stop when he have compose the number of line demand by the regulation unlike young swettenham for instance who with no more of poetry in his composition than mr
wakley yet would bring up a hundred dreary hexameter to the master after a half-holiday or young fluxmore who not only do his own verse but all the fifth form s besides
he never read to improve himself out of school-hour but on the contrary devour all the novel play and poetry on which he could lay his hand
he never be flogged but it be a wonder how he escape the whipping-post
when he have money he spend it royally in tart for himself and his friend he have be know to disburse nine and sixpence out of ten shilling award to he in a single day
when he have no fund he go on tick
when he could get no credit he go without and be almost as happy
he have be know to take a thrashing for a crony without say a word but a blow ever so slight from a friend would make he roar
to fight he be averse from his early youth as indeed to physic the greek grammar or any other exertion and would engage in none of they except at the last extremity
he seldom if ever tell lie and never bully little boy
those master or senior who be kind to he he love with boyish ardour
and though the doctor when he do not know his horace or could not construe his greek play say that that boy pendennis be a disgrace to the school a candidate for ruin in this world and perdition in the next a profligate who would most likely bring his venerable father to ruin and his mother to a dishonoured grave and the like--yet as the doctor make use of these compliment to most of the boy in the place which have not turn out an unusual number of felon and pickpocket little pen at first uneasy and terrify by these charge become gradually accustomed to hear they and he have not in fact either murder his parent or commit any act worthy of transportation or hang up to the present day
tutu there be many of the upper boy among the cistercians with whom pendennis be educate who assume all the privilege of man long before they quit that seminary
many of they for example smoke cigar--and some have already begin the practice of inebriation
one have fight a duel with an ensign in a marching in consequence of a row at the theatre-- another actually keep a buggy and horse at a livery stable in covent garden and might be see drive any sunday in hyde park with a groom with square arm and armorial button by his side
many of the senior be in love and show each other in confidence poem address to or letter and lock of hair receive from young lady--but pen a modest and timid youth rather envy these than imitate they as yet
he have not get beyond the theory as yet--the practice of life be all to come
and by the way ye tender mother and sober father of christian family a prodigious thing that theory of life be as orally learn at a great public school
why if you could hear those boy of fourteen who blush before mother and sneak off in silence in the presence of their daughter talk among each other--it would be the woman s turn to blush then
before he be twelve year old and if while his mother fancy he an angel of candour little pen have hear talk enough to make he quite awfully wise upon certain point--and so madam have your pretty little rosy-cheeked son who be come home from school for the ensue christmas holiday
i donot say that the boy be lose or that the innocence have leave he which he have from heaven which be our home but that the shade of the prison-house be close very fast over he and that we be help as much as possible to corrupt he
well--pen have just make his public appearance in a coat with a tail or cauda virili and be look most anxiously in his little study-glass to see if his whisker be grow like those of more fortunate youth his companion and instead of the treble voice with which he use to speak and sing for his singing voice be a very sweet one and he use when little to be make to perform home sweet home my pretty page and a french song or two which his mother have teach he and other ballad for the delectation of the senior boy have suddenly plunge into a deep bass diversify by a squeak which when he be call upon to construe in school set the master and scholar laugh he be about sixteen year old in a word when he be suddenly call away from his academic study
it be at the close of the forenoon school and pen have be unnoticed all the previous part of the morning till now when the doctor put he on to construe in a greek play
he do not know a word of it though little timmin his form-fellow be prompt he with all his might
pen have make a sad blunder or two when the awful chief break out upon he
pendennis sir he say your idleness be incorrigible and your stupidity beyond example
you be a disgrace to your school and to your family and i have no doubt will prove so in after-life to your country
if that vice sir which be describe to we as the root of all evil be really what moralist have represent and i have no doubt of the correctness of their opinion for what a prodigious quantity of future crime and wickedness be you unhappy boy lay the seed
miserable trifl
a boy who construe de and instead of de but at sixteen year of age be guilty not merely of folly and ignorance and dulness inconceivable but of crime of deadly crime of filial ingratitude which i tremble to contemplate
a boy sir who do not learn his greek play cheat the parent who spend money for his education
a boy who cheat his parent be not very far from rob or forge upon his neighbour
a man who forge on his neighbour pay the penalty of his crime at the gallow
and it be not such a one that i pity for he will be deservedly cut off but his maddened and heart-break parent who be drive to a premature grave by his crime or if they live drag on a wretched and dishonour old age
go on sir and i warn you that the very next mistake that you make shall subject you to the punishment of the rod
whobe that laugh
what ill-condition boy be there that dare to laugh shout the doctor
indeed while the master be make this oration there be a general titter behind he in the schoolroom
the orator have his back to the door of this ancient apartment which be open and a gentleman who be quite familiar with the place for both major arthur and mr
john pendennis have be at the school be ask the fifth-form boy who sate by the door for pendennis
the lad grinning point to the culprit against whom the doctor be pour out the thunder of his just wrath--major pendennis could not help laugh
he remember have stand under that very pillar where pen the young now stand and have be assault by the doctor s predecessor year and year ago
the intelligence be pass round that it be pendennis s uncle in an instant and a hundred young face wonder and giggling between terror and laughter turn now to the new-comer and then to the awful doctor
the major ask the fifth-form boy to carry his card up to the doctor which the lad do with an arch look
major pendennis have write on the card i must take a
p
home his father be very ill as the doctor receive the card and stop his harangue with rather a seared look the laughter of the boy half constrain until then burst out in a general shout
silence roar out the doctor stamp with his foot
pen look up and see who be his deliverer the major beckon to he gravely with one of his white glove and tumble down his book pen go across
the doctor take out his watch
it be two minute to one
we will take the juvenal at afternoon school he say nod to the captain and all the boy understand the signal gather up their book and pour out of the hall
young pen see by his uncle s face that something have happen at home
be there anything the matter with my mother he say
he could hardly speak though for emotion and the tear which be ready to start
no say the major but your father s very ill
go and pack your trunk directly i have get a postchaise at the gate pen go off quickly to his boarding-house to do as his uncle bid he and the doctor now leave alone in the schoolroom come out to shake hand with his old schoolfellow
you would not have think it be the same man
as cinderella at a particular hour become from a blazing and magnificent princess quite an ordinary little maid in a grey petticoat so as the clock strike one all the thunder majesty and awful wrath of the schoolmaster disappear
there be nothing serious i hope say the doctor
it be a pity to take the boy away unless there be
he be a very good boy rather idle and unenergetic but he be a very honest gentlemanlike little fellow though i cannot get he to construe as i wish
willnot you come in and have some luncheon
my wife will be very happy to see you but major pendennis decline the luncheon
he say his brother be very ill have have a fit the day before and it be a great question if they should see he alive
therebe no other son be there say the doctor
the major answer no and therebe a good eh--a good eh--property i believe ask the other in an off-hand way
h m--so so say the major
whereupon this colloquy come to an end
and arthur pendennis get into the postchaise with his uncle never to come back to school any more
as the chaise drive through clavering the hostler stand whistle under the archway of the clavering arms wink the postilion ominously as much as to say all be over
the gardener s wife come and open the lodge-gate and let the traveller through with a silent shake of the head
all the blind be down at fairoaks--the face of the old footman be as blank when he let they in
arthur s face be white too with terror more than with grief
whatever of warmth and love the deceased man might have have and he adore his wife and love and admire his son with all his heart he have shut they up within himself nor have the boy be ever able to penetrate that frigid outward barrier
but arthur have be his father s pride and glory through life and his name the last which john pendennis have try to articulate whilst he lie with his wife s hand clasp his own cold and clammy palm as the flickering spirit go out into the darkness of death and life and the world pass away from he
the little girl whose face have peer for a moment under the blind as the chaise come up open the door from the stair into the hall and take arthur s hand silently as he stoop down to kiss she lead he upstairs to his mother
old john open the dining-room door for the major
the room be darken with the blind down and surround by all the gloomy picture of the pendennises
he drink a glass of wine
the bottle have be open for the squire four day before
his hat be brush and lay on the hall table his newspaper and his letter-bag with john pendennis esquire fairoaks engrave upon the brass plate be there in waiting
the doctor and the lawyer from clavering who have see the chaise pass through come up in a gig half an hour after the major s arrival and enter by the back door
the former give a detailed account of the seizure and demise of mr
pendennis enlarge on his virtue and the estimation in which the neighbourhood hold he on what a loss he would be to the magistrate bench the county hospital etc
mrs
pendennis bear up wonderfully he say especially since master arthur s arrival
the lawyer stay and din with major pendennis and they talk business all the evening
the major be his brother s executor and joint guardian to the boy with mrs
pendennis
everything be leave unreservedly to she except in case of a second marriage an occasion which might offer itself in the case of so young and handsome a woman mr
tatham gallantly say when different provision be enact by the deceased
the major would of course take entire superintendence of everything under this most impressive and melancholy occasion
aware of this authority old john the footman when he bring major pendennis the candle to go to bed follow afterwards with the plate-basket and the next morning bring he the key of the hall clock--the squire always use to wind it up of a thursday john say
mrs
pendennis s maid bring he message from her mistress
she confirm the doctor s report of the comfort which master arthur s arrival have cause to his mother
what pass between that lady and the boy be not of import
a veil should be throw over those sacred emotion of love and grief
the maternal passion be a sacred mystery to i
what one see symbolise in the roman church in the image of the virgin mother with a bosom bleed with love i think one may witness and admire the almighty bounty for every day
i see a jewish lady only yesterday with a child at her knee and from whose face towards the child there shine a sweetness so angelical that it seem to form a sort of glory round both
i protest i could have kneel before she too and adore in her the divine beneficence in endow we with the maternal storge which begin with our race and sanctify the history of mankind
so it be with this in a word that mrs
pendennis comfort herself on the death of her husband whom however she always reverence as the good the most upright wise high-minded accomplished and awful of man
if the woman do not make idol of we and if they see we as we see each other would life be bearable or could society go on
let a man pray that none of his womankind should form a just estimation of he
if your wife know you as you be neighbour she would not grieve much about be your widow and would let your grave-lamp go out very soon or perhaps not even take the trouble to light it
whereas helen pendennis put up the handsome of memorial to her husband and constantly renew it with the most precious oil
as for arthur pendennis after that awful shock which the sight of his dead father must have produce on he and the pity and feeling which such an event no doubt occasion i be not sure that in the very moment of the grief and as he embrace his mother and tenderly console she and promise to love she for ever there be not spring up in his breast a feeling of secret triumph and exultation
he be the chief now and lord
he be pendennis and all round about he be his servant and handmaid
youwill never send i away little laura say trip by he and hold his hand
you willnot send i to school will you arthur arthur kiss she and pat her head
no she shouldnot go to school
as for go himself that be quite out of the question
he have determine that that part of his life should not be renew
in the midst of the general grief and the corpse still lie above he have leisure to conclude that he would have it all holiday for the future that he wouldnot get up till he like or stand the bullying of the doctor any more and have make a hundred of such day-dream and resolve for the future
how one s thought will travel
and how quickly our wish beget they
when he with laura in his hand go into the kitchen on his way to the dog-kennel the fowl-house and other his favourite haunt all the servant there assemble in great silence with their friend and the labour man and their wife and sally potter who go with the post-bag to clavering and the baker s man from clavering--all there assemble and drink beer on the melancholy occasion--rise up on his entrance and bow or curtsey to he
they never use to do so last holiday he feel at once and with indescribable pleasure
the cook cry out o lord and whisper how master arthur do grow thomas the groom in the act of drinking put down the jug alarm before his master
thomas s master feel the honour keenly
he go through and look at the pointer
as flora put her nose up to his waistcoat and ponto yell with pleasure hurtle at his chain pen patronise the dog and say poo ponto poo flora in his most condescending manner
and then he go and look at laura s hen and at the pig and at the orchard and at the dairy perhaps he blush to think that it be only last holiday he have in a manner rob the great apple-tree and be scold by the dairymaid for take cream
they bury john pendennis esquire formerly an eminent medical practitioner at bath and subsequently an able magistrate a benevolent landlord and a benefactor to many charity and public institution in this neighbourhood and county with one of the most handsome funeral that have be see since sir roger clavering be bury here the clerk say in the abbey church of clavering st
mary s
a fair marble slab from which the above inscription be copy be erect over the fairoaks pew in the church
on it you may see the pendennis coat of arm and crest an eagle look towards the sun with the motto nec tenui penna to the present day
doctor portman allude to the deceased most handsomely and affectingly as our dear depart friend in his sermon next sunday and arthur pendennis reign in his stead
chapter iii in which pendennis appear as a very young man indeed arthur be about sixteen year old we have say when he begin to reign in person for i see that the artist who be to illustrate this book and who make sad work of the likeness will never be able to take my friend off he have what his friend would call a dumpy but his mamma style a neat little figure
his hair be of a healthy brown colour which look like gold in the sunshine his face be round rosy freckle and good-humour his whisker when those facial ornament for which he sigh so ardently be award to he by nature be decidedly of a reddish hue in fact without be a beauty he have such a frank good-natured kind face and laugh so merrily at you out of his honest blue eye that no wonder mrs
pendennis think he the pride of the whole county
between the age of sixteen and eighteen he rise from five foot six to five foot eight inch in height at which altitude he pause
but his mother wonder at it
he be three inch tall than his father
be it possible that any man could grow to be three inch tall than mr
pendennis
you may be certain he never go back to school the discipline of the establishment do not suit he and he like be at home much well
the question of his return be debate and his uncle be for his go back
the doctor write his opinion that it be most important for arthur s success in after-life that he should know a greek play thoroughly but pen adroitly manage to hint to his mother what a dangerous place greyfriars be and what sad wild fellow some of the chap there be and the timid soul take alarm at once accede to his desire to stay at home
then pen s uncle offer to use his influence with his royal highness the commander-in-chief who be pleased to be very kind to he and propose to get pen a commission in the foot guards
pen s heart leap at this he have be to hear the band at st
james s play on a sunday when he go out to his uncle
he have see tom ricketts of the fourth form who use to wear a jacket and trouser so ludicrously tight that the eld boy could not forbear use he in the quality of a butt or cockshy he have see this very rickett array in crimson and gold with an immense bear-skin cap on his head stagger under the colour of the regiment
tom have recognise he and give he a patronising nod
tom a little wretch whom he have cut over the back with a hockey-stick last quarter--and there he be in the centre of the square rally round the flag of his country surround by bayonet crossbelt and scarlet the band blow trumpet and bang cymbal--talk familiarly to immense warrior with tuft to their chin and waterloo medal
what would not pen have give to wear such epaulette and enter such a service
but helen pendennis when this point be propose to she by her son put on a face full of terror and alarm
she say she do not quarrel with other who think differently but that in her opinion a christian have no right to make the army a profession
mr
pendennis never never would have permit his son to be a soldier
finally she should be very unhappy if he think of it now pen would have as soon cut off his nose and ear as deliberately and of aforethought malice make his mother unhappy and as he be of such a generous disposition that he would give away anything to any one he instantly make a present of his visionary red coat and epaulette and his ardour for military glory to his mother
she think he the noble creature in the world
but major pendennis when the offer of the commission be acknowledge and refuse write back a curt and somewhat angry letter to the widow and think his nephew be rather a spooney
he be content however when he see the boy s performance out hunt at christmas when the major come down as usual to fairoaks
pen have a very good mare and ride she with uncommon pluck and grace
he take his fence with great coolness and yet with judgment and without bravado
he write to the chap at school about his top-boot and his feat across country
he begin to think seriously of a scarlet coat and his mother must own that she think it would become he remarkably well though of course she pass hour of anguish during his absence and daily expect to see he bring home on a shutter
with these amusement in rather too great plenty it must not be assume that pen neglect his study altogether
he have a natural taste for read every possible kind of book which do not fall into his school-course
it be only when they force his head into the water of knowledge that he refuse to drink
he devour all the book at home from inchbald s theatre to white s farriery he ransack the neighbouring book-case
he find at clavering an old cargo of french novel which he read with all his might and he would sit for hour perch upon the topmost bar of doctor portman s library step with a folio on his knee whether it be hakluyt s travels hobbes s leviathan augustini opera or chaucer s poems
he and the vicar be very good friend and from his reverence pen learn that honest taste for port wine which distinguish he through life
and as for that dear good woman mrs
portman who be not in the least jealous though her doctor avow himself in love with mrs
pendennis whom he pronounce to be by far the fine lady in the county--all her grief be as she look up fondly at pen perch on the book-ladder that her daughter minny be too old for he--as indeed she be--miss myra portman be at that period only two year young than pen s mother and weigh as much as pen and mrs
pendennis together
be these detail insipid
look back good friend at your own youth and ask how be that
i like to think of a well-nurture boy brave and gentle warm-hearted and loving and look the world in the face with kind honest eye
what bright colour it wear then and how you enjoy it
a man have not many year of such time
he do not know they whilst they be with he
it be only when they be pass long away that he remember how dear and happy they be
in order to keep mr
pen from indulge in that idleness of which his friend the doctor of the cistercians have prophesy such awful consequence mr
smirke dr
portman s curate be engage at a liberal salary to walk or ride over from clavering and pass several hour daily with the young gentleman
smirke be a man perfectly faultless at a tea-table wear a curl on his fair forehead and tie his neck-cloth with a melancholy grace
he be a decent scholar and mathematician and teach pen as much as the lad be ever disposed to learn which be not much
for pen have soon take the measure of his tutor who when he come rid into the court-yard at fairoaks on his pony turn out his toe so absurdly and leave such a gap between his knee and the saddle that it be impossible for any lad endow with a sense of humour to respect such an equestrian
he nearly kill smirke with terror by put he on his mare and take he a ride over a common where the county fox-hound then bunt by that staunch old sportsman mr
hardhead of dumplingbeare happen to meet
mr
smirke on pen s mare rebecca she be name after pen s favourite heroine the daughter of isaac of york astound the hound as much as he disgust the huntsman lam one of the former by persist in rid amongst the pack and receive a speech from the latter more remarkable for energy of language than any oration he have ever hear since he leave the bargeman on the bank of isis
smirke confide to his pupil his poem both latin and english and present to mrs
pendennis a volume of the latter print at clapham his native place
the two read the ancient poet together and rattle through they at a pleasant rate very different from that steady grubbing pace with which the cistercians use to go over the classic ground scent out each word as they go and dig up every root in the way
pen never like to halt but make his tutor construe when he be at fault and thus gallop through the iliad and the odyssey the tragic playwriter writer and the charming wicked aristophanes whom he vow to be the great poet of all
but he go at such a pace that though he certainly gallop through a considerable extent of the ancient country he clean forget it in after-life and have only such a vague remembrance of his early classic course as a man have in the house of commons let we say who still keep up two or three quotation or a reviewer who just for decency s sake hint at a little greek
our people be the most prosaic in the world but the most faithful and with curious reverence we keep up and transmit from generation to generation the superstition of what we call the education of a gentleman
besides the ancient poet you may be sure pen read the english with great gusto
smirke sigh and shake his head sadly both about byron and moore
but pen be a swear fire-worshipper and a corsair he have they by heart and use to take little laura into the window and say zuleika i be not thy brother in tone so tragic that they cause the solemn little maid to open her great eye still wide
she sit until the proper hour for retirement sew at mrs
pendennis s knee and listen to pen read out to she of night without comprehend one word of what he read
he read shakspeare to his mother which she say she like but donot and byron and pope and his favourite lalla rookh which please she indifferently
but as for bishop heber and mrs
hemans above all this lady use to melt right away and be absorb into her pocket-handkerchief when pen read those author to she in his kind boyish voice
the christian year be a book which appear about that time
the son and the mother whisper it to each other with awe--faint very faint and seldom in after-life pendennis hear that solemn church-music but he always love the remembrance of it and of the time when it strike on his heart and he walk over the field full of hope and void of doubt as the church-bell rang on sunday morning
it be at this period of his existence that pen break out in the poet corner of the county chronicle with some verse with which he be perfectly well satisfied
his be the verse sign nep address to a tear on the anniversary of the battle of waterloo to madame caradori singing at the assize meetings on saint bartholomew s day a tremendous denunciation of popery and a solemn warning to the people of england to rally against emancipate the roman catholics etc etc all which masterpiece mrs
pendennis no doubt keep to this day along with his first sock the first cutting of his hair his bottle and other interesting relic of his infancy
he use to gallop rebecca over the neighbour dumpling down or into the county town which if you please we shall call chatteris spout his own poem and fill with quite a byronic afflatus as he think
his genius at this time be of a decidedly gloomy cast
he bring his mother a tragedy in which though he kill sixteen people before the second act it make she laugh so that he thrust the masterpiece into the fire in a pet
he project an epic poem in blank verse cortez or the conqueror of mexico and the inca s daughter he write part of seneca or the fatal bath and ariadne in naxos classical piece with chorus and strophe and antistrophe which sadly puzzle poor mrs
pendennis and begin a history of the jesuits in which he lash that order with tremendous severity and warn his protestant fellow-countryman of their machination
his loyalty do his mother s heart good to witness
he be a staunch unflinche church-and-king man in those day and at the election when sir giles beanfield stand on the blue interest against lord trehawk lord eyrie s son a whig and a friend of popery arthur pendennis with an immense bow for himself which his mother make and with a blue ribbon for rebecca ride alongside of the reverend doctor portman on his grey mare dowdy and at the head of the clavering voter whom the doctor bring up to plump for the protestant champion
on that day pen make his first speech at the blue hotel and also it appear for the first time in his life--take a little more wine than be good for he
mercy
what a scene it be at fairoaks when he ride back at ever so much o clock at night
what move about of lantern in the court-yard and stable though the moon be shin out what a gathering of servant as pen come home clatter over the bridge and up the stableyard with half a score of the clavering voter yell after he the blue song of the election
he want they all to come in and have some wine--some very good madeira some capital madeira--john go and get some madeira and there be no know what the farmer would have do have not madam pendennis make her appearance in a white wrapper with a candle--and scar those zealous blue so by the sight of her pale handsome face that they touch their hat and ride off
besides these amusement and occupation in which mr
pen indulge there be one which form the main business and pleasure of youth if the poet tell we aright whom pen be always study and this young fellow s heart be so ardent and his imagination so eager that it be not to be expect he should long escape the passion to which we allude and which lady you have rightly guess to be that of love
pen sigh for it first in secret and like the love-sick swain in ovid open his breast and say aura veni what generous youth be there that have not court some such windy mistress in his time
yes pen begin to feel the necessity of a first love--of a consume passion--of an object on which he could concentrate all those vague float fancy under which he sweetly suffer--of a young lady to whom he could really make verse and whom he could set up and adore in place of those unsubstantial ianthes and zuleikas to whom he address the outpouring of his gushing muse
he read his favourite poem over and over again he call upon alma venus the delight of god and man he translate anacreon s ode and pick out passage suitable to his complaint from waller dryden prior and the like
smirke and he be never weary in their interview of discourse about love
the faithless tutor entertain he with sentimental conversation in place of lecture on algebra and greek for smirke be in love too
who could help it be in daily intercourse with such a woman
smirke be madly in love as far as such a mild flame as mr
smirke s may be call madness with mrs
pendennis
that honest lady sit down below stair teach little laura to play the piano or devise flannel petticoat for the poor round about she or otherwise busy with the calm routine of her modest and spotless christian life be little aware what storm be brew in two bosom upstairs in the study--in pen s as he sate in his shooting jacket with his elbow on the green study-table and his hand clutch his curly brown hair homer under his nose and in worthy mr
smirke s with whom he be read
here they would talk about helen and andromache
andromache s like my mother pen use to avouch but i say smirke by jove iwould cut off my nose to see helen and he would spout certain favourite line which the reader will find in their proper place in the third book
he draw portrait of she--they be extant still--with straight nose and enormous eye and arthur pendennis delineavit et pinxit gallantly write underneath
as for mr
smirke he naturally prefer andromache
and in consequence he be uncommonly kind to pen
he give he his elzevir horace of which the boy be fond and his little greek testament which his own mamma at clapham have purchase and present to he
he buy he a silver pencil-case and in the matter of learn let he do just as much or as little as ever he please
he always seem to be on the point of unbosom himself to pen nay he confess to the latter that he have a--an attachment an ardently cherish attachment about which pendennis long to hear and say tell we old chap be she handsome
have she get blue eye or black but doctor portman s curate heave a gentle sigh cast up his eye to the ceiling and beg pen faintly to change the conversation
poor smirke
he invite pen to dine at his lodging over madame fribsby s the milliner s in clavering and once when it be rain and mrs
pendennis who have drive in her pony-chaise into clavering with respect to some arrangement about leave off mourning probably be prevail upon to enter the curate s apartment he send out for pound-cake instantly
the sofa on which she sate become sacred to he from that day and he keep flower in the glass which she drink from ever after
as mrs
pendennis be never tired of hear the praise of her son we may be certain that this rogue of a tutor neglect no opportunity of converse with she upon that subject
it might be a little tedious to he to hear the story about pen s generosity about his bravery in fight the big naughty boy about his fun and joke about his prodigious skill in latin music riding etc but what price would he not pay to be in her company
and the widow after these conversation think mr
smirke a very pleasing and well-inform man
as for her son she have not settle in her mind whether he be to be senior wrangler and archbishop of canterbury or double first class at oxford and lord chancellor
that all england do not possess his peer be a fact about which there be in her mind no manner of question
a simple person of inexpensive habit she begin forthwith to save and perhaps to be a little parsimonious in favour of her boy
there be no entertainment of course at fairoaks during the year of her weed
nor indeed do the doctor s silver dish-cover of which he be so proud and which be flourish all over with the arm of the pendennises and surmount with their crest come out of the plate-chest again for long long year
the household be diminish and its expense curtail
there be a very blank anchorite repast when pen din from home and he himself head the remonstrance from the kitchen regard the deteriorated quality of the fairoaks beer
she be become miserly for pen
indeed who ever accuse woman of be just
they be always sacrifice themselves or somebody for somebody else s sake
there happen to be no young woman in the small circle of friend who be in the widow s intimacy whom pendennis could by any possibility gratify by endow she with the inestimable treasure of a heart which he be long to give away
some young fellow in this predicament bestow their young affection upon dolly the dairymaid or cast the eye of tenderness upon molly the blacksmith s daughter
pen think a pendennis much too grand a personage to stoop so low
he be too high-minded for a vulgar intrigue and at the idea of an intrigue or a seduction have he ever entertain it his heart would have revolt as from the notion of any act of baseness or dishonour
miss minny portman be too old too large and too fond of read rollin s ancient history the miss boardbacks admiral boardback s daughter of st
vincent s or fourth of june house as it be call disgust pen with the london air which they bring into the country from gloucester place where they pass the season and look down upon pen as a chit
captain glanders s h p 50 th dragoon guards three girl be in brown-holland pinafore as yet with the end of their hair-plait tie up in dirty pink ribbon
not have acquire the art of dancing the youth avoid such chance as he might have have of meeting with the fair sex at the chatteris assembly in fine he be not in love because there be nobody at hand to fall in love with
and the young monkey use to ride out day after day in quest of dulcinea and peep into the pony-chaise and gentlefolk carriage as they drive along the broad turnpike road with a heart beat within he and a secret tremor and hope that she might be in that yellow postchaise come swing up the hill or one of those three girl in beaver bonnet in the back seat of the double gig which the fat old gentleman in black be drive at four mile an hour
the postchaise contain a snuffy old dowager of seventy with a maid her contemporary
the three girl in the beaver bonnet be no handsomer than the turnip that skirt the roadside
do as he might and ride where he would the fairy princess that he be to rescue and win have not yet appear to honest pen
upon these point he do not discourse to his mother
he have a world of his own
what generous ardent imaginative soul have not a secret pleasure-place in which it disport
let no clumsy prying or dull meddling of ours try to disturb it in our child
actaeon be a brute for want to push in where diana be bath
leave he occasionally alone my good madam if you have a poet for a child
even your admirable advice may be a bore sometimes

yonder little child may have thought too deep even for your great mind and fancy so coy and timid that they will not bare themselves when your ladyship sit by
helen pendennis by the force of sheer love divine a great number of her son s secret

while these natural sentiment be wag war and trouble in honest pen s bosom it chance one day that he ride into chatteris for the purpose of carry to the county chronicle a tremendous and thrilling poem for the next week s paper and put up his horse accord to custom at the stable of the george hotel there he fall in with an old acquaintance

anybody who have ever see one of our great light comedian x in a chintz dressing-gown such as nobody ever wear and represent himself to the public as a young nobleman in his apartment and while away the time with light literature until his friend sir harry shall arrive or his father shall come down to breakfast--anybody i say who have see the great x

those who have only see miss fotheringay in later day since her marriage and introduction into london life have little idea how beautiful a creature she be at the time when our friend pen first set eye on she and i warn my reader as beforehand that the pencil which illustrate this work and can draw an ugly face tolerably well but be sadly put out when it try to delineate a beauty can give no sort of notion of she
she be of the tall of woman and at she then age of six-and-twenty-for six-and-twenty she be though she vow she be only nineteen--in the prime and fulness of her beauty
her forehead be vast and her black hair wave over it with a natural ripple that beauty of late day have try to imitate with the help of the crimping-iron and be confine in shin and voluminous braid at the back of a neck such as you see on the shoulder of the louvre venus--that delight of god and man
her eye when she lift they up to gaze on you and ere she drop their purple deep-fringe lid shine with tenderness and mystery unfathomable
love and genius seem to look out from they and then retire coyly as if ashamed to have be see at the lattice
who could have have such a command brow but a woman of high intellect
she never laugh indeed her tooth be not good but a smile of endless tenderness and sweetness play round her beautiful lip and in the dimple of her cheek and her lovely chin
her nose defy description in those day
her ear be like two little pearl shell which the earring she wear though the handsome property in the theatre only insult
she be dress in long flow robe of black which she manage and sweep to and fro with wonderful grace and out of the fold of which you only see her sandal occasionally they be of rather a large size but pen think they as ravish as the slipper of cinderella
but it be her hand and arm that this magnificent creature most excel in and somehow you could never see she but through they
they surround she
when she fold they over her bosom in resignation when she drop they in mute agony or raise they in superb command when in sportive gaiety her hand flutter and wave before she like what shall we say like the snowy dove before the chariot of venus--it be with these arm and hand that she beckon repel entreat embrace her admirer--no single one for she be arm with her own virtue and with her father s valour whose sword would have leapt from its scabbard at any insult offer to his child--but the whole house which rise to she as the phrase be as she curtsey and bow and charm it

what become of he subsequently to his retirement from the army be no affair of our
i take it no foreigner understand the life of an irish gentleman without money the way in which he manage to keep afloat--the wind-raise conspiracy in which he engage with hero as unfortunate as himself--the mean by which he contrive during most day of the week to get his portion of whisky-and-water all these be mystery to we inconceivable but suffice it to say that through all the storm of life jack have float somehow and the lamp of his nose have never go out

those who remember this grand actress on the stage can recall how she use always precisely the same gesture look and tone how she stand on the same plank of the stage in the same position roll her eye at the same instant and to the same degree and weep with precisely the same heart-rend pathos and over the same pathetic syllable
and after she have come out tremble with emotion before the audience and look so exhausted and tearful that you fancy she would faint with sensibility she would gather up her hair the instant she be behind the curtain and go home to a mutton-chop and a glass of brown stout and the harrowing labour of the day over she go to bed and snore as resolutely and as regularly as a porter
