chapter vii in which the major make his appearance our acquaintance major arthur pendennis arrive in due time at fairoaks after a dreary night pass in the mail-coach where a stout fellow-passenger swell preternaturally with great-coat have crowd he into a corner and keep he awake by snore indecently where a widow lady opposite have not only shut out the fresh air by close all the window of the vehicle but have fill the interior with fume of jamaica rum and water which she suck perpetually from a bottle in her reticule where whenever he catch a brief moment of sleep the twanging of the horn at the turnpike-gate or the scuffling of his huge neighbour wedge he close and close or the play of the widow s foot on his own tender toe speedily wake up the poor gentleman to the horror and reality of life--a life which have pass away now and become impossible and only live in fond memory
eight mile an hour for twenty or five-and-twenty hour a tight mail-coach a hard seat a gouty tendency a perpetual change of coachman grumble because you do not fee they enough a fellow-passenger partial to spirit-and-water who have not bear with these evil in the jolly old time
and how could people travel under such difficulty
and yet they do and be merry too
next the widow and by the side of the major s servant on the roof be a couple of school-boy go home for the midsummer holiday and major pendennis wonder to see they sup at the inn at bagshot where they take in a cargo of ham eggs pie pickle tea coffee and boil beef which surprise the poor major sip a cup of very feeble tea and think with a tender dejection that lord steyne s dinner be come off at that very moment
the ingenuous ardour of the boy however amuse the major who be very good-natured and he become the more interested when he find that the one who travel inside with he be a lord s son whose noble father pendennis of course have meet in the world of fashion which he frequent
the little lord sleep all night through in spite of the squeezing and the horn-blowing and the widow and he look as fresh as paint and indeed pronounce himself to be so when the major with a yellow face a bristly beard a wig out of curl and strong rheumatic grief shoot through various limb of his uneasy body descend at the little lodge-gate at fairoaks where the porteress and gardener s wife reverentially greet he and still more respectfully mr
morgan his man
helen be on the look-out for this expected guest and see he from her window
but she do not come forward immediately to greet he
she know the major do not like to be see at a surprise and require a little preparation before he care to be visible
pen when a boy have incur sad disgrace by carry off from the major s dressing-table a little morocco box which it must be confess contain the major s back tooth which he naturally would leave out of his jaw in a jolting mail-coach and without which he would not choose to appear
morgan his man make a mystery of mystery of his wig curl they in private place introduce they mysteriously to his master s room nor without his head of hair would the major care to show himself to any member of his family or any acquaintance
he go to his apartment then and supply these deficiency he groan and moan and wheeze and curse morgan through his toilet as an old buck will who have be up all night with a rheumatism and have a long duty to perform
and finally be belt curl and set straight he descend upon the drawing-room with a grave majestic air such as befit one who be at once a man of business and a man of fashion
pen be not there however only helen and little laura sew at her knee and to whom he never present more than a forefinger as he do on this occasion after salute his sister-in-law

lead on laura pen say with a half fierce half comic air-- lead on and say i wait upon my uncle but he be laugh in order to hide a great anxiety and be screw his courage inwardly to face the ordeal which he know be now before he
pen have take smirke into his confidence in the last two day and after the outbreak attendant on the discovery of doctor portman and during every one of those forty-eight hour which he have pass in mr
smirke s society have do nothing but talk to his tutor about miss fotheringay-- miss emily fotheringay--emily etc to all which talk smirke listen without difficulty for he be in love himself most anxious in all thing to propitiate pen and indeed very much himself enrapture by the personal charm of this goddess whose like never have be before at a theatrical representation he have not behold until now
pen s fire and volubility his hot eloquence and rich poetical trope and figure his manly heart kind ardent and hopeful refuse to see any defect in the person he love any difficulty in their position that he might not overcome have half convince mr
smirke that the arrangement propose by mr
pen be a very feasible and prudent one and that it would be a great comfort to have emily settle at fairoaks captain costigan in the yellow room establish for life there and pen marry at eighteen
and it be a fact that in these two day the boy have almost talk over his mother too have parry all her objection one after another with that indignant good sense which be often the perfection of absurdity and have bring she almost to acquiesce in the belief that if the marriage be doom in heaven why doom it be--that if the young woman be a good person it be all that she for her part have to ask and rather to dread the arrival of the guardian uncle who she foresaw would regard mr
pen s marriage in a manner very different to that simple romantic honest and utterly absurd way in which the widow be already dispose to look at question of this sort
for as in the old allegory of the gold and silver shield about which the two knight quarrel each be right accord to the point from which he look so about marriage the question whether it be foolish or good wise or otherwise depend upon the point of view from which you regard it
if it mean a snug house in belgravia and pretty little dinner-party and a pretty little brougham to drive in the park and a decent provision not only for the young people but for the little belgravians to come and if these be the necessary of life and they be with many honest people to talk of any other arrangement be an absurdity of love in lodging--a babyish folly of affection that cannot pay coach-hire or afford a decent milliner--as mere wicked balderdash and childish romance
if on the other hand your opinion be that people not with an assured subsistence but with a fair chance to obtain it and with the stimulus of hope health and strong affection may take the chance of fortune for well or bad and share its good or its evil together the polite theory then become an absurdity in its turn bad than an absurdity a blasphemy almost and doubt of providence and a man who wait to make his choose woman happy until he can drive she to church in a neat little carriage with a pair of horse be no well than a coward or a trifl who be neither worthy of love nor of fortune
i donot say that the town folk be not right but helen pendennis be a country-breed woman and the book of life as she interpret it tell she a different story to that page which be read in city
like most soft and sentimental woman matchmaking in general form a great part of her thought and i daresay she have begin to speculate about her son s fall in love and marry long before the subject have ever enter into the brain of the young gentleman
it please she with that dismal pleasure which the idea of sacrifice themselves give to certain woman to think of the day when she would give up all to pen and he should bring his wife home and she would surrender the key and the good bedroom and go and sit at the side of the table and see he happy
what do she want in life but to see the lad prosper
as an empress certainly be not too good for he and would be honour by become mrs

chapter ix in which the major open the campaign let those who have a real and heartfelt relish for london society and the privilege of an entree into its most select circle admit that major pendennis be a man of no ordinary generosity and affection in the sacrifice which he now make
he give up london in may his newspaper and his morning--his afternoon from club to club his little confidential visit to my lady his ride in rotten row his dinner and his stall at the opera his rapid escapade to fulham or richmond on saturdays and sundays his bow from my lord duke or my lord marquis at the great london entertainment and his name in the morning post of the succeed day his quieter little festival more select secret and delightful--all these he resign to lock himself into a lone little country house with a simple widow and a greenhorn of a son a mawkish curate and a little girl of ten year of age
he make the sacrifice and it be the great that few know the extent of it
his letter come down frank from town and he show the invitation to helen with a sigh
it be beautiful and tragical to see he refuse one party after another--at least to those who could understand as helen donot the melancholy grandeur of his self-denial
helen do not or only smile at the awful pathos with which the major speak of the court guide in general but young pen look with great respect at the great name upon the superscription of his uncle s letter and listen to the major s story about the fashionable world with constant interest and sympathy

he dress himself laboriously every morning and afternoon he take regular exercise up and down the terrace walk
thus with his cane his toilet his medicine-chest his backgammon-box and his newspaper this worthy and worldly philosopher fence himself against ennui and if he do not improve each shin hour like the bee by the widow s garden wall major pendennis make one hour after another pass as he could and render his captivity just tolerable
after this period it be remark that he be fond of bring round the conversation to the american war the massacre of wyoming and the brilliant action of saint lucie the fact be that he have a couple of volume of the annual register in his bedroom which he sedulously study
it be thus a well-regulate man will accommodate himself to circumstance and show himself calmly superior to fortune

but pen do not drown himself as perhaps his mother fancy might be his intention
he like to go and fish there and think and think at leisure as the float quiver in the little eddy of the pond and the fish flap about he
if he get a bite he be excited enough and in this way occasionally bring home carps tench and eel which the major cook in the continental fashion
by this pond and under a tree which be his favourite resort pen compose a number of poem suitable to his circumstance over which verse he blush in after day wonder how he could ever have invent such rubbish
and as for the tree why it be in a hollow of this very tree where he use to put his tin-box of ground-bait and other fishing commodity that he afterwards--but we be advance matter

when a man s grief or passion be at this point it may be loud but it be not very severe

there be hold at an inn in that county town a weekly meeting of a festive almost a riotous character of a society of gentleman who call themselves the buccaneer
some of the choice spirit of chatteris belong to this cheerful club
graf the apothecary than whom a well fellow never put a pipe in his mouth and smoke it smart the talented and humorous portrait-painter of high street croker an excellent auctioneer and the uncompromise hicks the able editor for twenty-three year of the county chronicle and chatteris champion be amongst the crew of the buccaneer whom also bingley the manager like to join of a saturday evening whenever he receive permission from his lady
costigan have be also an occasional buccaneer
but a want of punctuality of payment have of late somewhat exclude he from the society where he be subject to disagreeable remark from the landlord who say that a buccaneer who donot pay his shot be utterly unworthy to be a marine bandit
but when it become know to the ear as the clubbists call themselves familiarly that miss fotheringay have make a splendid engagement a great revolution of feeling take place in the club regard captain costigan
solly mine host of the grape and i need not say as worthy a fellow as ever stand behind a bar tell the gent in the buccaneer room one night how noble the captain have behave have be round and pay off all his tick in chatteris include his score of three pound fourteen here--and pronounce that cos be a good feller a gentleman at bottom and he solly have always say so and finally work upon the feeling of the buccaneer to give the captain a dinner

pen s eye drink in with an eagerness which belong to first impression and major pendennis survey with that calmness which belong to a gentleman who do not care for the picturesque and whose eye have be somewhat dim by the constant glare of the pavement of pall mall
saint george s be the great college of the university of oxbridge with its four vast quadrangle and its beautiful hall and garden and the georgians as the man be call wear gown of a peculiar cut and give themselves no small air of superiority over all other young man
little saint boniface be but a petty hermitage in comparison of the huge consecrate pile alongside of which it lie
but consider its size it have always keep an excellent name in the university
its ton be very good the good family of certain county have time out of mind send up their young man to saint boniface the college living be remarkably good the fellowship easy the boniface man have have more than their fair share of university honour their boat be third upon the river their chapel-choir be not inferior to saint george s itself and the boniface ale the good in oxbridge
in the comfortable old wainscote college-hall and round about roubilliac s statue of saint boniface who stand in an attitude of seraphic benediction over the uncommonly good cheer of the fellow table there be portrait of many most eminent bonifacians
there be the learned doctor griddle who suffer in henry viii s time and archbishop bush who roast he--there be lord chief justice hicks-- the duke of st
david s k g chancellor of the university and member of this college--sprott the poet of whose fame the college be justly proud doctor blogg the late master and friend of doctor johnson who visit he at saint boniface--and other lawyer scholar and divine whose portraiture look from the wall or whose coat-of-arm shine in emerald and ruby gold and azure in the tall window of the refectory
the venerable cook of the college be one of the good artist in oxbridge his son take the high honour in the other university of camford and the wine in the fellow room have long be fame for its excellence and abundance
into this certainly not the least snugly sheltered arbour amongst the grove of academe pen now find his way lean on his uncle s arm and they speedily reach mr

arthur pendennis say he should be happy to give he the good set of room to be have in college--a gentleman-pensioner s set indeed which be just luckily vacant
so they part until dinner-time which be very near at hand and major pendennis pronounce mr
buck to be uncommonly civil indeed
indeed when a college magnate take the trouble to be polite there be no man more splendidly courteous
immerse in their book and exclude from the world by the gravity of their occupation these reverend man assume a solemn magnificence of compliment in which they rustle and swell as in their grand robe of state
those silk and brocade be not put on for all comer or every day
when the two gentleman have take leave of the tutor in his study and have return to mr

and he inspect the entire costume with a great deal of satisfaction in one of the great gilt mirror which ornament mr

the major smile as he see the boy dandify himself in the glass the old gentleman be not displeased with the appearance of the comely lad

spicer

then they go to hall where pen sate down and eat his common with his brother freshman and the major take his place at the high-table along with the college dignitary and other father or guardian of youth who have come up with their son to oxbridge and after hall they go to mr
buck s to take wine and after wine to chapel where the major sate with great gravity in the upper place have a fine view of the master in his carve throne or stall under the organ-loft where that gentleman the learn doctor donne sate magnificent with his great prayer-book before he an image of statuesque piety and rigid devotion
all the young freshman behave with gravity and decorum but pen be shock to see that atrocious little foker who come in very late and half a dozen of his comrade in the gentleman-pensioner seat giggle and talk as if they have be in so many stall at the opera
but these circumstance it must be remember take place some year back when william the fourth be king
young man be much well behave now and besides saint boniface be rather a fast college
pen could hardly sleep at night in his bedroom at the trencher so anxious be he to begin his college life and to get into his own apartment
what do he think about as he lie tossing and awake
be it about his mother at home the pious soul whose life be bind up in his
yes let we hope he think of she a little

the six month passion have age he very considerably

chapter xix pendennis of boniface our friend pen be not sorry when his mentor take leave of the young gentleman on the second day after the arrival of the pair in oxbridge and we may be sure that the major on his part be very glad to have discharge his duty and to have the duty over
more than three month of precious time have that martyr of a major give up to his nephew--be ever selfish man call upon to make a great sacrifice
do you know many man or major who would do as much
a man will lay down his head or peril his life for his honour but let we be shy how we ask he to give up his ease or his heart s desire
very few of we can bear that trial
say worthy reader if thou hast peradventure a beard wouldst thou do as much
i will not say that a woman will not
they be use to it we take care to accustom they to sacrifice but my good sir the amount of self-denial which you have probably exert through life when put down to your account elsewhere will not probably swell the balance on the credit side much
well well there be no use in speaking of such ugly matter and you be too polite to use a vulgar to quoque
but i wish to state once for all that i greatly admire the major for his conduct during the past quarter and think that he have quite a right to be pleased at get a holiday

the major rush off to london and thence to cheltenham from which watering-place he descend upon some neighbour great house whereof the family be not go abroad and where good shooting and company be to be have
a quarter of the space which custom have award to work style the serial nature have be assign to the account of one passage in pen s career and it be manifest that the whole of his adventure cannot be treat at a similar length unless some descendant of the chronicler of pen s history should take up the pen at his decease and continue the narrative for the successor of the present generation of reader
we be not about to go through the young fellow s academical career with by any mean a similar minuteness
alas the life of such boy do not bear tell altogether
i wish it do
i ask you do yours
as long as what we call our honour be clear i suppose your mind be pretty easy
woman be pure but not man
woman be unselfish but not man
and i would not wish to say of poor arthur pendennis that he be bad than his neighbour only that his neighbour be bad for the most part
let we have the candour to own as much at least

mine be pretty large but i cannot find ten saint in the list
during the first term of mr
pen s academical life he attend classical and mathematical lecture with tolerable assiduity but discover before very long time that he have little taste or genius for the pursuing of the exact science and be perhaps rather annoyed that one or two very vulgar young man who do not even use strap to their trouser so as to cover the abominably thick and coarse shoe and stocking which they wear beat he completely in the lecture-room he give up his attendance at that course and announce to his fond parent that he propose to devote himself exclusively to the cultivation of greek and roman literature
mrs
pendennis be for her part quite satisfied that her darling boy should pursue that branch of learn for which he have the great inclination and only besought he not to ruin his health by too much study for she have hear the most melancholy story of young student who by over-fatigue have bring on brain-fever and perish untimely in the midst of their university career
and pen s health which be always delicate be to be regard as she justly say beyond all consideration or vain honour
pen although not aware of any lurking disease which be likely to end his life yet kindly promise his mamma not to sit up read too late of night and stick to his word in this respect with a great deal more tenacity of resolution than he exhibit upon some other occasion when perhaps he be a little remiss
presently he begin too to find that he learn little good in the classical lecture
his fellow-student there be too dull as in mathematic they be too learn for he
mr

binge his bed-maker and pen grow weary of hear the dull student and tutor blunder through a few line of a play which he could read in a tenth part of the time which they give to it
after all private reading as he begin to perceive be the only study which be really profitable to a man and he announce to his mamma that he should read by himself a great deal more and in public a great deal less
that excellent woman know no more about homer than she do about algebra but she be quite content with pen s arrangement regard his course of study and feel perfectly confident that her dear boy would get the place which he merit
pen do not come home until after christmas a little to the fond mother s disappointment and laura s who be long for he to make a fine snow fortification such as he have make three winter before
but he be invite to logwood lady agnes foker s where there be private theatrical and a gay christmas party of very fine folk some of they whom major pendennis would on no account have his nephew neglect
however he stay at home for the last three week of the vacation and laura have the opportunity of remark what a quantity of fine new clothe he bring with he and his mother admire his improved appearance and manly and decide tone

and he have a new french watch and gold chain in place of the big old chronometer with its bunch of jingle seal which have hang from the fob of john pendennis and by the second-hand of which the defunct doctor have feel many a patient s pulse in his time

this portly and valuable chronometer pen now pronounce to be out of date and indeed make some comparison between it and a warming-pan which laura think disrespectful and he leave the watch in a drawer in the company of soiled primrose glove cravat which have go out of favour and of that other school watch which have once before be mention in this history

as he be smoke to such good purpose his mother could not of course refuse permission in fact the good soul come into the room one day in the midst of pen s labour he be consult a novel which have recently appear for the cultivation of the light literature of his own country as well as of foreign nation become every student helen we say come into the room and find pen on the sofa at this work rather than disturb he go for a light-box and his cigar-case to his bedroom which be adjacent and actually put the cigar into his mouth and light the match at which he kindle it

arthur s own allowance be liberal all this time indeed much more so than those of the son of far more wealthy man

let he be liberally allowance be major pendennis s maxim
let he make his first entree into the world as a gentleman and take his place with man of good rank and station after give it to he it will be his own duty to hold it
there be no such bad policy as stint a boy--or put he on a low allowance than his fellow
arthur will have to face the world and fight for himself presently
meanwhile we shall have procure for he good friend gentlemanly habit and have he well back and well train against the time when the real struggle come
and these liberal opinion the major probably advance both because they be just and because he be not deal with his own money
thus young pen the only son of an estated country gentleman with a good allowance and a gentlemanlike bearing and person look to be a lad of much more consequence than he be really and be hold by the oxbridge authority tradesman and undergraduate as quite a young buck and member of the aristocracy
his manner be frank brave and perhaps a little impertinent as become a high-spirited youth
he be perfectly generous and free-handed with his money which seem pretty plentiful
he love joviality and have a good voice for a song
boat-racing have not rise in pen s time to the fureur which as we be give to understand it have since attain in the university and rid and tandem-driving be the fashion of the ingenuous youth
pen ride well to hound appear in pink as become a young buck and not particularly extravagant in equestrian or any other amusement yet manage to run up a fine bill at nile s the livery-stable keeper and in a number of other quarter
in fact this lucky young gentleman have almost every taste to a considerable degree
he be very fond of book of all sort doctor portman have teach he to like rare edition and his own taste lead he to like beautiful binding
it be marvellous what tall copy and gilding and marbling and blind-tooling the bookseller and binder put upon pen s bookshelf
he have a very fair taste in matter of art and a keen relish for print of a high school--none of your french opera dancers or tawdry race prints such as have delight the simple eye of mr
spicer his predecessor--but your stranges and rembrandt etching and wilkies before the letter with which his apartment be furnish presently in the most perfect good taste as be allow in the university where this young fellow get no small reputation
we have mention that he exhibit a certain partiality for ring jewellery and fine raiment of all sort and it must be own that mr
pen during his time at the university be rather a dressy man and love to array himself in splendour
he and his polite friend would dress themselves out with as much care in order to go and dine at each other s room as other folk would who be go to enslave a mistress
they say he use to wear ring over his kid glove which he always deny but what folly will not youth perpetrate with its own admirable gravity and simplicity
that he take perfume bath be a truth and he use to say that he take they after meet certain man of a very low set in hall

he show they in confidence the verse that he have write to she and his brow would darken his eye roll his chest heave with emotion as he recall that fatal period of his life and describe the woe and agony which he have suffer
the verse be copy out hand about sneer at admire pass from coterie to coterie
there be few thing which elevate a lad in the estimation of his brother boy more than to have a character for a great and romantic passion
perhaps there be something noble in it at all time--among very young man it be consider heroic--pen be pronounce a tremendous fellow
they say he have almost commit suicide that he have fight a duel with a baronet about she

man use to say that they have be walk with pendennis and be as pleased to be see in his company as some of we would be if we walk with a duke down pall mall
he and the proctor cap each other as they meet as if they be rival power and the man hardly know which be the great
in fact in the course of his second year arthur pendennis have become one of the man of fashion in the university
it be curious to watch that facile admiration and simple fidelity of youth
they hang round a leader and wonder at he and love he and imitate he
no generous boy ever live i suppose that have not have some wonderment of admiration for another boy and monsieur pen at oxbridge have his school his faithful band of friend and his rival
when the young man hear at the haberdasher shop that mr
pendennis of boniface have just order a crimson satin-cravat you would see a couple of dozen crimson satin cravat in main street in the course of the week--and simon the jeweller be know to sell no less than two gross of pendennis pin from a pattern which the young gentleman have select in his shop
now if any person with an arithmetical turn of mind will take the trouble to calculate what a sum of money it would cost a young man to indulge freely in all the above propensity which we have say mr
pen possess it will be see that a young fellow with such liberal taste and amusement must need in the course of two or three year spend or owe a very handsome sum of money
we have say our friend pen have not a calculate turn
no one propensity of his be outrageously extravagant and it be certain that paddington s tailor s account guttlebury s cook s bill for dinner dillon tandy s bill with finn the print seller for raphael-morgheus and landseer proof and wormall s dealing with parkton the great bookseller for aldine edition black-letter folio and richly illuminate missals of the xvi
century and snaffle s or foker s score with nile the horsedealer be each and all of they incomparably great than any little bill which mr

but pendennis of boniface have the advantage over all these young gentleman his friend and associate of a universality of taste and whereas young lord paddington do not care twopence for the most beautiful print or to look into any gilt frame that have not a mirror within it and guttlebury do not mind in the least how he be dress and have an aversion for horse exercise nay a terror of it and snaffle never read any print work but the racing calendar or bell s life or care for any manuscript except his greasy little scrawl of a bet-book our catholic-minded young friend occupy himself in every one of the branch of science or pleasure above-mention and distinguish himself tolerably in each
hence young pen get a prodigious reputation in the university and be hail as a sort of crichton and as for the english verse prize in competition for which we have see he busily engage at fairoaks jones of jesus carry it that year certainly but the undergraduate think pen s a much fine poem and he have his verse print at his own expense and distribute in gilt morocco cover amongst his acquaintance
i find a copy of it lately in a dusty corner of mr
pen s bookcase and have it before i this minute bind up in a collection of old oxbridge tract university statute prize-poem by successful and unsuccessful candidate declamation recite in the college chapel speech deliver at the union debating society and inscribe by arthur with his name and college pendennis--boniface or present to he by his affectionate friend thompson or jackson the author
how strange the epigraph look in those half-boyish hand and what a thrill the sight of the document give one after the lapse of a few lustre
how fate since that time have remove some estranged other deal awfully with all
many a hand be cold that write those kindly memorial and that we press in the confident and generous grasp of youthful friendship
what passion our friendship be in those old day how artless and void of doubt
how the arm you be never tired of have link in yours under the fair college avenue or by the river side where it wash magdalen gardens or christ church meadows or wind by trinity and king s be withdraw of necessity when you enter presently the world and each part to push and struggle for himself through the great mob on the way through life
be we the same man now that write those inscription--that read those poem
that deliver or hear those essay and speech so simple so pompous so ludicrously solemn parody so artlessly from book and speak with smug chubby face and such an admirable aping of wisdom and gravity
here be the book before i it be scarcely fifteen year old
here be jack moan with despair and byronic misanthropy whose career at the university be one of unmixed milk-punch
here be tom s daring essay in defence of suicide and of republicanism in general apropos of the death of roland and the girondins--tom s who wear the starchest tie in all the diocese and would go to smithfield rather than eat a beefsteak on a friday in lent
here be bob of the circuit who have make a fortune in railroad committees and whose dinner be so good bellowing out with tancred and godfrey on to the breach ye soldier of the cross scale the red wall and swim the choke foss
ye dauntless archer twang your cross-bows well on bill and battle-axe and mangonel
ply battering-ram and hurtling catapult jerusalem be ours--id deus vult after which come a mellifluous description of the garden of sharon and the maid of salem and a prophecy that rose shall deck the entire country of syria and a speedy reign of peace be establish--all in undeniably decasyllabic line and the queer aping of sense and sentiment and poetry
and there be essays and poems along with these grave parody and boyish exercise which be at once so frank and false and mirthful yet somehow so mournful by youthful hand that shall never write more
fate have interpose darkly and the young voice be silent and the eager brain have cease to work
this one have genius and a great descent and seem to be destine for honour which now be of little worth to he that have virtue learning genius--every faculty and endowment which might secure love admiration and worldly fame an obscure and solitary churchyard contain the grave of many fond hope and the pathetic stone which bid they farewell--i see the sun shin on it in the fall of last year and hear the sweet village choir raise anthem round about
what boot whether it be westminster or a little country spire which cover your ash or if a few day soon or later the world forget you
amidst these friend then and a host more pen pass more than two brilliant and happy year of his life
he have his fill of pleasure and popularity
no dinner- or supper-party be complete without he and pen s jovial wit and pen s song and dash courage and frank and manly bearing charm all the undergraduate and even disarm the tutor who cry out at his idleness and murmur about his extravagant way of life
though he become the favourite and leader of young man who be much his superior in wealth and station he be much too generous to endeavour to propitiate they by any meanness or cringe on his own part and would not neglect the humble man of his acquaintance in order to curry favour with the rich young grandee in the university
his name be still remember at the union debating club as one of the brilliant orator of his day
by the way from have be an ardent tory in his freshman s year his principle take a sudden turn afterwards and he become a liberal of the most violent order
he avow himself a dantonist and assert that louis the sixteenth be serve right
and as for charles the first he vow that he would chop off that monarch s head with his own right hand be he then in the room at the union debating club and have cromwell no other executioner for the traitor
he and lord magnus charters the marquis of runnymede s son before-mention be the most truculent republican of their day
there be reputation of this sort make quite independent of the collegiate hierarchy in the republic of gownsman
a man may be famous in the honour-list and entirely unknown to the undergraduate who elect king and chieftain of their own whom they admire and obey as negro-gang have private black sovereign in their own body to whom they pay an occult obedience besides that which they publicly profess for their owner and driver
among the young one pen become famous and popular not that he do much but there be a general determination that he could do a great deal if he choose
ah if pendennis of boniface would but try the man say he might do anything he be back for the greek ode win by smith of trinity everybody be sure he would have the latin hexameter prize which brown of st
john s however carry off and in this way one university honour after another be lose by he until after two or three failure mr
pen cease to compete
but he get a declamation prize in his own college and bring home to his mother and laura at fairoaks a set of prize-book begilt with the college arm and so big well-bind and magnificent that these lady think there have be no such prize ever give in a college before as this of pen s and that he have win the very large honour which oxbridge be capable of award
as vacation after vacation and term after term pass away without the desire news that pen have sate for any scholarship or win any honour doctor portman grow mightily gloomy in his behaviour towards arthur and adopt a sulky grandeur of deportment towards he which the lad return by a similar haughtiness
one vacation he do not call upon the doctor at all much to his mother s annoyance who think that it be a privilege to enter the rectory-house at clavering and listen to dr
portman s antique joke and story though ever so often repeat with unfaile veneration
i cannot stand the doctor s patronising air pen say
hebe too kind to i a great deal fatherly
i have see in the world well man than he and be not go to bore myself by listen to his dull old story and drink his stupid old port wine the tacit feud between pen and the doctor make the widow nervous so that she too avoid portman and be afraid to go to the rectory when arthur be at home
one sunday in the last long vacation the wretched boy push his rebellious spirit so far as not to go to church and he be see at the gate of the clavering arms smoke a cigar in the face of the congregation as it issue from st
mary s
there be an awful sensation in the village society portman prophesy pen s ruin after that and groan in spirit over the rebellious young prodigal
so do helen tremble in her heart and little laura--laura have grow to be a fine young stripling by this time graceful and fair cling round helen and worship she with a passionate affection
both of these woman feel that their boy be change
he be no long the artless pen of old day so brave so artless so impetuous and tender
his face look careworn and haggard his voice have a deep sound and tone more sarcastic
care seem to be pursue he but he only laugh when his mother question he and parry her anxious query with some scornful jest
nor do he spend much of his vacation at home he go on visit to one great friend or another and scar the quiet pair at fairoaks by story of great house whither he have be invite and by talk of lord without their title
honest harry foker who have be the mean of introduce arthur pendennis to that set of young man at the university from whose society and connexion arthur s uncle expect that the lad would get so much benefit who have call for arthur s first song at his first supper-party and who have present he at the barmecide club where none but the very good man of oxbridge be admit it consist in pen s time of six nobleman eight gentleman-pensioner and twelve of the most select commoner of the university soon find himself leave far behind by the young freshman in the fashionable world of oxbridge and be a generous and worthy fellow without a spark of envy in his composition be exceedingly pleased at the success of his young protege and admire pen quite as much as any of the other youth do
i be he who follow pen now and quote his saying learn his song and retail they at minor supper-party and be never weary of hear they from the gifted young poet s own mouth--for a good deal of the time which mr
pen might have employ much more advantageously in the pursuit of the regular scholastic study be give up to the composition of secular ballad which he sing about at party accord to university willnot
it have be as well for arthur if the honest foker have remain for some time at college for with all his vivacity he be a prudent young man and often curb pen s propensity to extravagance but foker s collegiate career do not last very long after arthur s entrance at boniface
repeat difference with the university authority cause mr
foker to quit oxbridge in an untimely manner
he would persist in attend race on the neighbouring hungerford heath in spite of the injunction of his academic superior
he never could be get to frequent the chapel of the college with that regularity of piety which alma mater demand from her child tandem which be abomination in the eye of the head and tutor be foker s great delight and so reckless be his driving and frequent the accident and upset out of his drag that pen call take a drive with he take the diversion of purley finally have a dinner-party at his room to entertain some friend from london nothing would satisfy mr
foker but paint mr
buck s door vermilion in which freak he be catch by the proctor and although young black strap the celebrate negro fighter who be one of mr
foker s distinguished guest and be hold the can of paint while the young artist operate on the door knock down two of the proctor s attendant and perform prodigy of valour yet these feat rather injure than serve foker whom the proctor know very well and who be take with the brush in his hand and who be summarily convene and send down from the university
the tutor write a very kind and feel letter to lady agnes on the subject state that everybody be fond of the youth that he never mean harm to any mortal creature that he for his own part would have be delighted to pardon the harmless little boyish frolic have not its unhappy publicity render it impossible to look the freak over and breathe the most fervent wish for the young fellow s welfare--wish no doubt sincere for foker as we know come of a noble family on his mother s side and on the other be heir to a great number of thousand pound a year
it donot matter say foker talk over the matter with pen a little soon or a little later what be the odd
i should have be pluck for my little-go again i know i should--that latin i cannot screw into my head and my mamma s anguish would have break out next term
the governor will blow like an old grampus i know he will well we must stop till he get his wind again
i shall probably go abroad and improve my mind with foreign travel
yes parly-voo s the ticket
it ly and that sort of thing
iwill go to paris and learn to dance and complete my education
but itbe not i ibe anxious about pen
as long as people drink beer i donot care it s about you ibe doubtful my boy
yoube go too fast and cannot keep up the pace i tell you
itbe not the fifty you owe me pay it or not when you like but itbe the every-day pace and i tell you it will kill you
yoube livin as if there be no end to the money in the stockin at home
you oughtnot to give dinner you ought to eat them
fellow be glad to have you
you oughtnot to owe horse bill you ought to ride other chap nag
you know no more about bet than i do about algebra the chap will win your money as sure as you sport it
hang i if you be not try everything
i see you sit down to ecarte last week at trumpington s and take your turn with the bone after ringwood s supper
theywill beat you at it pen my boy even if they play on the square which
i donot say they donot nor which i donot say they do mind
but i willnot play with them
yoube no match for them
you benot up to their weight
itbe like little black strap stand up to tom spring the blackbe a pretty fighter but law bless you his arm benot long enough to touch tom and i tell you yoube go it with feller beyond your weight
look here--if youwill promise i never to bet nor touch a box nor a card iwill let you off the two pony but pen laughingly say that though it benot convenient to he to pay the two pony at that moment he by no mean wish to be let off any just debt he owe and he and foker part not without many dark foreboding on the latter s part with regard to his friend who harry think be travel speedily on the road to ruin
one must do at rome as rome do pen say in a dandified manner jingle some sovereign in his waistcoat-pocket
a little quiet play at ecarte cannot hurt a man who play pretty well--i come away fourteen sovereign rich from ringwood s supper and gad
i want the money and he walk off after have take leave of poor foker who go away without any beat of drum or offer to drive the coach out of oxbridge to superintend a little dinner which he be go to give at his own room in boniface about which dinner the cook of the college who have a great respect for mr
pendennis always take especial pain for his young favourite
chapter xx rake s progress some short time before mr
foker s departure from oxbridge there have come up to boniface a gentleman who have once as it turn out belong to the other university of camford which he have quit on account of some difference with the tutor and authority there
this gentleman whose name be horace bloundell be of the ancient suffolk family of bloundell-bloundell of bloundell-bloundell hall bloundell-bloundellshire as the young wag use to call it and no doubt it be on account of his descent and because dr
donne the master of boniface be a suffolk man and relate perhaps to the family that mr
horace bloundell be take in at boniface after st
george s and one or two other colleges have refuse to receive he
there be a living in the family which it be important for mr
bloundell to hold and be in a dragoon regiment at the time when his third brother for whom the living be originally intend sicken and die mr
bloundell determine upon quit crimson pantaloon and sable shakos for the black coat and white neckcloth of the english divine
the misfortune which occur at camford occasion some slight disturbance to mr
bloundell s plan but although defeat upon one occasion the resolute ex-dragoon be not dismayed and set to work to win a victory elsewhere
in pen s second year major pendennis pay a brief visit to his nephew and be introduce to several of pen s university friend--the gentle and polite lord plinlimmon the gallant and open-hearted magnus charters the sly and witty harland the intrepid ringwood who be call rupert in the union debating club from his opinion and the bravery of his blunder broadbent style barebones broadbent from the republican nature of his opinion he be of a dissent family from bristol and a perfect boanerge of debate mr
bloundell-bloundell finally who have at once take his place among the select of the university
major pendennis though he do not understand harland s greek quotation or quite appreciate broadbent s thick shoe and dingy hand be nevertheless delighted with the company assemble round his nephew and highly approve of all the young man with the exception of that one who give himself the great air in the society and affect most to have the manner of a man of the world
as he and pen sate at breakfast on the morning after the party in the room of the latter the major give his opinion regard the young man with whom he be in the great good-humour
he have regale they with some of his story which though not quite so fresh in london where people have a diseased appetite for novelty in the way of anecdote be entirely new at oxbridge and the lad hear they with that honest sympathy that eager pleasure that boisterous laughter or that profound respect so rare in the metropolis and which must be so delightful to the profess raconteur
only once or twice during the telling of the anecdote mr
bloundell s face wear a look of scorn or betray by its expression that he be acquaint with the tale narrate
once he have the audacity to question the accuracy of one of the particular of a tale as give by major pendennis and give his own version of the anecdote about which he know he be right for he hear it openly talk of at the club by so-and-so and t other who be present at the business
the youngster present look up with wonder at their associate who dare to interrupt the major--few of they could appreciate that melancholy grace and politeness with which major pendennis at once accede to mr
bloundell s version of the story and thank he for correct his own error
they star on the next occasion of meeting when bloundell speak in contemptuous term of old pen say everybody know old pen regular old trencherman at gaunt house notorious old bore regular old fogy
major pendennis on his side like mr
bloundell not a whit
these sympathy be pretty sure to be mutual amongst man and woman and if for my part some kind friend tell i that such and such a man have be abuse i i be almost sure on my own side that i have a misliking to such and such a man
we like or dislike each other as folk like or dislike the odour of certain flower or the taste of certain dish or wine or certain book
we cannot tell why--but as a general rule all the reason in the world will not make we love dr
fell and as sure as we dislike he we may be sure that he dislike we
so the major say pen my boy your dinner go off a merveille you do the honour very nicely--you carve well--i be glad you learn to carve--it be do on the sideboard now in most good house but be still an important point and may aid you in middle-life--young lord plinlimmon be a very amiable young man quite the image of his dear mother whom i know as lady aquila brownbill and lord magnus s republicanism will wear off--it sit prettily enough on a young patrician in early life though nothing be so loathsome among person of our rank--mr
broadbent seem to have much eloquence and considerable read your friend foker be always delightful but your acquaintance mr
bloundell strike i as in all respect a most ineligible young man bless my soul sir bloundell-bloundell cry pen laughing why sir hebe the most popular man of the university
we elect he of the barmecides the first week he come up--have a special meeting on purpose-- hebe of an excellent family--suffolk bloundells descend from richard s blondel bear a harp in chief--and motto o mong roy a man may have a very good coat-of-arm and be a tiger my boy the major say chip his egg that man be a tiger mark my word--a low man
i will lay a wager that he leave his regiment which be a good one for a more respectable man than my friend lord martingale never sate in a saddle in bad odour
there be the unmistakable look of slang and bad habit about this mr
bloundell
he frequent low gambling-house and billiard-hell sir--he haunt third-rate club--i know he do
i know by his style
i never be mistaken in my man yet
do you remark the quantity of ring and jewellery he wear
that person have scamp write on his countenance if any man ever have
mark my word and avoid he
let we turn the conversation
the dinner be a leetle too fine but i donot object to your make a few extra frais when you receive friend
of course you donot do it often and only those whom it be your interest to feter
the cutlet be excellent and the souffle uncommonly light and good
the third bottle of champagne be not necessary but you have a good income and as long as you keep within it i shall not quarrel with you my dear boy poor pen
the worthy uncle little know how often those dinner take place while the reckless young amphitryon delight to show his hospitality and skill in gourmandise
there be no art than that so long to learn so difficult to acquire so impossible and beyond the mean of many unhappy people about which boy be more anxious to have an air of knowingness
a taste and knowledge of wine and cookery appear to they to be the sign of an accomplished roue and manly gentleman
i like to see they wink at a glass of claret as if they have an intimate acquaintance with it and discuss a salmi--poor boy--it be only when they grow old that they know they know nothing of the science when perhaps their conscience whisper they that the science be in itself little worth and that a leg of mutton and content be as good as the dinner of pontiff
but little pen in his character of admirable crichton think it necessary to be a great judge and practitioner of dinner we have just say how the college cook respect he and shall soon have to deplore that that worthy man so blindly trust our pen
in the third year of the lad s residence at oxbridge his staircase be by no mean encumber with dish-cover and dessert and waiter carry in dish and skip open iced champagne crowd of different sort of attendant with face sulky or piteous hang about the outer oak and assail the unfortunate lad as he issue out of his den
nor do his guardian s advice take any effect or induce mr
pen to avoid the society of the disreputable mr
bloundell
what young man like in their companion be what have get pen a great part of his own repute and popularity a real or suppose knowledge of life
a man who have see the world or can speak of it with a knowing air--a roue or lovelace who have his adventure to relate be sure of an admire audience among boy
it be hard to confess but so it be
we respect that sort of prowess
from our school-day we have be teach to admire it
be there five in the hundred out of the hundred and hundred of english school-boy bring up at our great school and college that must not own at one time of their life to have read and like don juan
awful propagation of evil the idea of it should make the man tremble who hold the pen lest untruth or impurity or unjust anger or unjust praise escape it
one such diseased creature as this be enough to infect a whole colony and the tutor of boniface begin to find the moral tone of their college lower and their young man grow unruly and almost ungentleman-like soon after mr
bloundell s arrival at oxbridge
the young magnate of the neighbour great college of st
george s who regard pen and in whose society he live be not take in by bloundell s flashy grace and rakish air of fashion
broadbent call he captain macheath and say he would live to be hang
foker during his brief stay at the university with macheath with characteristic caution decline to say anything in the captain s disfavour but hint to pen that he have well have he for a partner at whist than play against he and well back he at ecarte than bet on the other side
you see he play well than you do pen be the astute young gentleman s remark he play uncommon well the captain does and pen i wouldnot take the odd too freely from he if i be you
i donot think hebe too flush of money the captain benot but beyond these dark suggestion and generality the cautious foker could not be get to speak
not that his advice would have have more weight with a headstrong young man than advice commonly have with a lad who be determined on pursue his own way
pen s appetite for pleasure be insatiable and he rush at it wherever it present itself with an eagerness which bespoke his fiery constitution and youthful health
he call take pleasure see life and quote well-know maxim from terence from horace from shakspeare to show that one should do all that might become a man
he bid fair to be utterly use up and a roue in a few year if he be to continue at the pace at which he be go
one night after a supper-party in college at which pen and macheath have be present and at which a little quiet vingt-et-un have be play an amusement much pleasanter to man in their second and third year than the boisterous custom of sing song which bring the proctor about the room and which have grow quite stale by this time every man have expend his budget as the man have take their cap and be go away after no great loss or winning on any side mr
bloundell playfully take up a green wine-glass from the supper-table which have be destine to contain iced cup but into which he insert something still more pernicious namely a pair of dice which the gentleman take out of his waistcoat-pocket and put into the glass
then give the glass a graceful wave which show that his hand be quite experienced in the throwing of dice he call seven the main and whisk the ivory cube gently on the table sweep they up lightly again from the cloth and repeat this process two or three time
the other man look on pen of course among the number who have never use the dice as yet except to play a humdrum game of backgammon at home
mr
bloundell who have a good voice begin to troll out the chorus from robert the devil an opera then in great vogue in which chorus many of the man join especially pen who be in very high spirit have win a good number of shilling and half-crown at the vingt-et-un--and presently instead of go home most of the party be seat round the table play at dice the green glass go round from hand to hand until pen finally shiver it after throw six main
from that night pen plunge into the delight of the game of hazard as eagerly as it be his custom to pursue any new pleasure
dice can be play of morning as well as after dinner or supper
bloundell would come into pen s room after breakfast and it be astonish how quick the time pass as the bone be rattle
they have little quiet party with closed door and bloundell devise a box line with felt so that the dice should make no noise and their tell-tale rattle not bring the sharp-eare tutor up to the room
bloundell ringwood and pen be once very nearly catch by mr
buck who pass in the quadrangle think he hear the word two to one on the caster through pen s open window but when the tutor get into arthur s room he find the lad with three homer before they and pen say he be try to coach the two other man and ask mr
buck with great gravity what be the present condition of the river scamander and whether it be navigable or no
mr
arthur pendennis do not win much money in these transaction with mr
bloundell or indeed gain good of any kind except a knowledge of the odd at hazard which he might have learn out of book
captain macheath have other accomplishment which he exercise for pen s benefit
the captain s story have a great and unfortunate charm for arthur who be never tired of hear bloundell s history of garrison conquest and of his feat in country-quarters he have be at paris and have plenty of legend about the palais royal and the salon and frascati s
he have go to the salon one night after a dinner at the cafe de paris when we be all devilishly cut by jove and on wake in the morning in my own room i find myself with twelve thousand franc under my pillow and a hundred and forty-nine napoleons in one of my boot
benot that a coup hay the captain say
pen s eye glisten with excitement as he hear this story
he respect the man who could win such a sum of money
he sigh and say it would set he all right
macheath laugh and tell he to drink another drop of maraschino
i could tell you story much more wonderful than that he add and so indeed the captain could have do without any further trouble than that of invention with which portion of the poetic faculty nature have copiously endow he
he laugh to scorn pen s love for miss fotheringay when he come to hear of that amour from arthur as he pretty soon do for we have say pen be not averse to tell the story now to his confidential friend and he and they be rather proud of the transaction
but macheath take away all pen s conceit on this head not by demonstrate the folly of the lad s passion for an uneducated woman much his senior in year but by expose his absurd desire of gratify his passion in a legitimate way
marry she say he you might as well marry and he name one of the most notorious actress on the stage
she havenot a shred of a character he know twenty man who be openly admirer of she and name they and the sum each have spend upon she
i know no kind of calumny more frightful or frequent than this which take away the character of woman no man more reckless and mischievous than those who lightly use it and no kind of coward more despicable than the people who invent these slander
be it or not a misfortune that a man himself of a candid disposition and dispose like our friend pen to blurt out the truth on all occasion begin life by believe all that be say to he
would it be well for a lad to be less trustful and so less honest
it require no small experience of the world to know that a man who have no especial reason thereto be tell you lie
i be not sure whether it be not good to go on be dupe for a certain time
at all event our honest pen have a natural credulity which enable he to accept all statement which be make to he and he take every one of captain macheath s figment as if they have be the most unquestioned fact of history
so bloundell s account about miss fotheringay pain and mortify pen exceedingly
if he have be ashamed of his passion before what be his feeling regard it now when the object of so much pure flame and adoration turn out to be only a worthless impostor an impostor detect by all but he
it never occur to pen to doubt the fact or to question whether the story of a man who like his new friend never speak well of any woman be likely to be true
one easter vacation when pen have announce to his mother and uncle his intention not to go down but stay at oxbridge and read mr
pen be nevertheless induce to take a brief visit to london in company with his friend mr
bloundell
they put up at a hotel in covent garden where bloundell have a tick as he call it and take the pleasure of the town very freely after the willnot of young university man
bloundell still belong to a military club whither he take pen to dine once or twice the young man would drive thither in a cab tremble lest they should meet major pendennis on his beat in pall mall and here pen be introduce to a number of gallant young fellow with spur and mustachio with whom he drink pale-ale of morning and beat the town of a night
here he see a deal of life indeed nor in his career about the theatre and singing-house which these roar young blade frequent be he very likely to meet his guardian
one night nevertheless they be very near to each other a plank only separate pen who be in the box of the museum theatre from the major who be in lord steyne s box along with that venerate nobleman
the fotheringay be in the pride of her glory
shad make a hit that is she have draw very good house for nearly a year have star the province with great eclat have come back to shine in london with somewhat diminish lustre and now be act with ever increase attraction etc triumph of the good old british drama as the play-bill avow to house in which there be plenty of room for anybody who want to see she
it be not the first time pen have see she since that memorable day when the two have part in chatteris
in the previous year when the town be make much of she and the press laud her beauty pen have find a pretext for come to london in term-time and have rush off to the theatre to see his old flame
he recollect it rather than renew it
he remember how ardently he use to be on the look-out at chatteris when the speech before ophelia s or mrs
haller s entrance on the stage be make by the proper actor
now as the actor speak he have a sort of feeble thrill as the house begin to thunder with applause and ophelia enter with her old bow and sweeping curtsey pen feel a slight shock and blush very much as he look at she and could not help think that all the house be regard he
he hardly hear she for the first part of the play and he think with such rage of the humiliation to which she have subject he that he begin to fancy he be jealous and in love with she still
but that illusion do not last very long
he run round to the stage-door of the theatre to see she if possible but he do not succeed
she pass indeed under his nose with a female companion but he do not know her nor do she recognise he
the next night he come in late and stay very quietly for the afterpiece and on the third and last night of his stay in london--why taglioni be go to dance at the opera taglioni
and there be to be don giovanni which he admire of all thing in the world so mr
pen go to don giovanni and taglioni
this time the illusion about she be quite gone
she be not less handsome but she be not the same somehow
the light be go out of her eye which use to flash there or pen s no long be dazzle by it
the rich voice speak as of old yet it do not make pen s bosom thrill as formerly
he think he could recognise the brogue underneath the accent seem to he coarse and false
it annoy he to hear the same emphasis on the same word only utter a little louder bad than this it annoy he to think that he should ever have mistake that loud imitation for genius or melt at those mechanical sob and sigh
he feel that it be in another life almost that it be another man who have so madly love she
he be ashamed and bitterly humiliate and very lonely
ah poor pen
the delusion be well than the truth sometimes and fine dream than dismal waking
they go and have an uproarious supper that night and mr
pen have a fine headache the next morning with which he go back to oxbridge have spend all his ready money
as all this narrative be take from pen s own confession so that the reader may be assure of the truth of every word of it and as pen himself never have any accurate notion of the manner in which he spend his money and plunge himself in much deep pecuniary difficulty during his luckless residence at oxbridge university it be of course impossible for i to give any accurate account of his involvement beyond that general notion of his way of life which have be sketch a few page back
he do not speak too hardly of the roguery of the university tradesman or of those in london whom he honour with his patronage at the outset of his career
even finch the money-lender to whom bloundell introduce he and with whom he have various transaction in which the young rascal s signature appear upon stamp paper treat he accord to pen s own account with forbearance and never mulct he of more than a hundred per cent
the old college-cook his fervent admirer make he a private bill offer to send he in dinner up to the very last and never would have press his account to his die day
there be that kindness and frankness about arthur pendennis which win most people who come in contact with he and which if it render he an easy prey to rogue get he perhaps more goodwill than he merit from many honest man
it be impossible to resist his good-nature or in his bad moment not to hope for his rescue from utter ruin
at the time of his full career of university pleasure he would leave the gay party to go and sit with a sick friend
he never know the difference between small and great in the treatment of his acquaintance however much the unlucky lad s taste which be of the sumptuous order lead he to prefer good society he be only too ready to share his guinea with a poor friend and when he get money have an irresistible propensity for pay which he never could conquer through life
in his third year at college the dun begin to gather awfully round about he and there be a levee at his oak which scandalise the tutor and would have scar many a stouter heart
with some of these he use to battle some he would bully under mr
bloundell s direction who be a master in this art though he take a degree in no other and some deprecate
and it be report of he that little mary frodsham the daughter of a certain poor gilder and frame-maker whom mr
pen have think fit to employ and who have make a number of beautiful frame for his fine print come to pendennis with a piteous tale that her father be ill with ague and that there be an execution in their house pen in an anguish of remorse rush away pawn his grand watch and every single article of jewellery except two old gold sleeve-button which have belong to his father and rush with the proceed to frodsham s shop where with tear in his eye and the deep repentance and humility he ask the poor tradesman s pardon
this young gentleman be not tell as an instance of pen s virtue but rather of his weakness
it would have be much more virtuous to have have no print at all
he still stand for the bauble which he sell in order to pay frodsham s bill and his mother have cruelly to pinch herself in order to discharge the jeweller s account so that she be in the end the sufferer by the lad s impertinent fancy and folly
we be not present pen to you as a hero or a model only as a lad who in the midst of a thousand vanity and weakness have as yet some generous impulse and be not altogether dishonest
we have say it be to the scandal of mr
buck the tutor that pen s extravagance become know from the manner in which he enter college the associate he keep and the introduction of doctor portman and the major buck for a long time think that his pupil be a man of large property and wonder rather that he only wear a plain gown
once on go up to london to the levee with an address from his majesty s loyal university of oxbridge buck have see major pendennis at st
james s in conversation with two knight of the garter in the carriage of one of whom the dazzle tutor see the major whisk away after the levee
he ask pen to wine the instant he come back let he off from chapel and lecture more than ever and feel perfectly sure that he be a young gentleman of large estate
thus he be thunderstruck when he hear the truth and receive a dismal confession from pen
his university debt be large and the tutor have nothing to do and of course pen do not acquaint he with his london debt
what man ever do tell all when press by his friend about his liability
the tutor learn enough to know that pen be poor that he have spend a handsome almost a magnificent allowance and have raise around he such a fine crop of debt as it would be very hard work for any man to mow down for there be no plant that grow so rapidly when once it have take root
perhaps it be because she be so tender and good that pen be terrify lest his mother should know of his sin
i cannot bear to break it to she he say to the tutor in an agony of grief
o
sir ihave be a villain to her and he repent and he wish he have the time to come over again and he ask himself why why do his uncle insist upon the necessity of live with great people and in how much do all his grand acquaintance profit he they be not shy but pen think they be and slunk from they during his last term at college
he be as gloomy as a death head at party which he avoid of his own part or to which his young friend soon cease to invite he
everybody know that pendennis be hard up that man bloundell who could pay nobody and who be oblige to go down after three term be his ruin the man say
his melancholy figure might be see shirk about the lonely quadrangle in his battered old cap and tear gown and he who have be the pride of the university but a year before the man whom all the young one love to look at be now the object of conversation at freshman s wine-party and they speak of he with wonder and awe
at last come the degree examinations
many a young man of his year whose hob-nail shoe pen have deride and whose face or coat he have caricature--many a man whom he have treat with scorn in the lecture-room or crush with his eloquence in the debating-club--many of his own set who have not half his brain but a little regularity and constancy of occupation take high place in the honour or pass with decent credit
and where in the list be pen the superb pen the wit and dandy pen the poet and orator

there must be some sort of pleasure which we man donot understand which accompany the pain of be scarify and indeed i believe some woman would rather actually so suffer than not
they like sacrifice themselves in behalf of the object which their instinct teach they to love

there be scarce a man that read this but have administer pleasure in this fashion to his womankind and have treat they to the luxury of forgive he

during the latter part of pen s residence at the university of oxbridge his uncle s partiality have greatly increase for the lad

i be not good at description of female beauty and indeed do not care for it in the least think that goodness and virtue be of course far more advantageous to a young lady than any mere fleeting charm of person and face and so shall not attempt any particular delineation of miss laura bell at the age of sixteen year
at that age she have attain her present altitude of five foot four inch so that she be call tall and gawky by some and a maypole by other of her own sex who prefer littler woman
but if she be a maypole she have beautiful rose about her head and it be a fact that many swain be dispose to dance round she
she be ordinarily pale with a faint rose tinge in her cheek but they flush up in a minute when occasion call and continue so blush ever so long the rose remain after the emotion have pass away which have summon those pretty flower into existence
her eye have be describe as very large from her early childhood and retain that characteristic in later life
good-natured critic always female say that she be in the habit of make play with those eye and ogle the gentleman and lady in her company but the fact be that nature have make they so to shine and to look and they could no more help so look and shin than one star can help be bright than another
it be doubtless to mitigate their brightness that miss laura s eye be provide with two pair of veil in the shape of the long and fine black eyelash so that when she close her eye the same people who find fault with those orb say that she want to show her eyelash off and indeed i daresay that to see her asleep would have be a pretty sight
as for her complexion that be nearly as brilliant as lady mantrap s and without the powder which her ladyship use
her nose must be leave to the reader s imaginaton if her mouth be rather large as miss piminy aver who but for her know appetite one would think could not swallow anything large than a button everybody allow that her smile be charming and show off a set of pearly tooth whilst her voice be so low and sweet that to hear it be like listen to sweet music
because she be in the habit of wear very long dress people of course say that her foot be not small but it may be that they be of the size become her figure and it do not follow because mrs
pincher be always put her foot out that all other lady should be perpetually bring theirs on the tapis
in fine miss laura bell at the age of sixteen be a sweet young lady
many thousand of such be to be find let we hope in this country where there be no lack of goodness and modesty and purity and beauty

you know mamma this young lady say that i have be live with you for ten year during which time you have never take any of my money and have be treat i just as if i be a charity girl
now this obligation have offend i very much because i be proud and do not like to be beholden to people

rowdy with the bald head and ask he for it not for his head but for the five hundred pound and i dare say he will send you two more which we will save and pay back and we will send the money to pen who can pay all his debt without hurt anybody and then we will live happy ever after what helen reply to this speech need not be repeat as the widow s answer be make up of a great number of incoherent ejaculation embrace and other irrelative matter
but the two woman sleep well after that talk and when the night-lamp go out with a splutter and the sun rise gloriously over the purple hill and the bird begin to sing and pipe cheerfully amidst the leafless tree and glisten evergreen on fairoaks lawn helen wake too and as she look at the sweet face of the girl sleep beside she her lip part with a smile blush on her cheek her spotless bosom heaving and fall with gentle undulation as if happy dream be sweep over it--pen s mother feel happy and grateful beyond all power of word save such as pious woman offer up to the beneficent dispenser of love and mercy--in whose honour a chorus of such praise be constantly rise up all round the world

pen s remorse and so determine his plan of economy that he would not take an inside place in the coach but sate up behind with his friend the guard who remember his former liberality and lend he plenty of great-coat

old john be in wait to receive his master s baggage but he appear in a fustian jacket and no long wear his livery of drab and blue

we may be sure that the widow have a certain other object in view have lose no time in write off to pen an account of the noble the magnanimous the magnificent offer of laura fill up her letter with a profusion of benediction upon both her child

so the prodigal come home and the fat calf be kill for he and he be make as happy as two simple woman could make he
no allusion be make to the oxbridge mishap or question ask as to his farth proceeding for some time
but pen debate these anxiously in his own mind and up in his own room where he pass much time in cogitation
a few day after he come home he ride to chatteris on his horse and come back on the top of the coach
he then inform his mother that he have leave the horse to be sell and when that operation be effect he hand she over the cheque which she and possibly pen himself think be an act of uncommon virtue and self-denial but which laura pronounce to be only strict justice
he rarely mention the loan which she have make and which indeed have be accept by the widow with certain modification but once or twice and with great hesitation and stammering he allude to it and thank she but it evidently pain his vanity to be beholden to the orphan for succour
he be wild to find some mean of repay she
he leave off drink wine and betook himself but with great moderation to the refreshment of whisky-and-water
he give up cigar-smoking but it must be confess that of late year he have like pipe and tobacco as well or even well so that this sacrifice be not a very severe one
he fall asleep a great deal after dinner when he join the lady in the drawing-room and be certainly very moody and melancholy
he watch the coach with great interest walk in to read the paper at clavering assiduously din with anybody who would ask he and the widow be glad that he should have any entertainment in their solitary place and play a good deal at cribbage with captain glanders

portman who in his turn whenever pen pass give he very severe look from under his shovel-hat
he go to church with his mother however very regularly and read prayer for she at home to the little household
always humble it be greatly diminish now a couple of maid do the work of the house of fairoaks the silver dish-cover never see the light at all
john put on his livery to go to church and assert his dignity on sundays but it be only for form s sake
he be gardener and out-door man vice upton resign
there be but little fire in fairoaks kitchen and john and the maid drink their evening beer there by the light of a single candle
all this be mr
pen s doing and the state of thing do not increas his cheerfulness

pen go
a plucked man be a dismal being in a university belong to no set of man there and own by no one
pen feel himself pluck indeed of all the fine feather which he have win during his brilliant year and rarely appear out of his college regularly go to morning chapel and shut himself up in his room of night away from the noise and supper of the undergraduate

the man of his year have take their degree and be go
he go into a second examination and pass with perfect ease
he be somewhat more easy in his mind when he appear in his bachelor s gown

he call a second time but morgan the valet say his master be from home
