it be dull--very certainly
the tender widow s habitual melancholy seem to deepen into a sad gloom and laura see with alarm that the dear friend become every year more languid and weary and that her pale cheek grow more wan

just about the time of pen s little mishap and when he be so absorb in the grief occasion by that calamity as to take no notice of event which befell person less interesting to himself than arthur pendennis an announcement appear in the provincial journal which cause no small sensation in the county at least and in all the town village hall and mansion and parsonage for many mile round clavering park
at clavering market at cackleby fair at chatteris sessions on gooseberry green as the squire s carriage meet the vicar s one-horse contrivance and the inmate of both vehicle stop on the road to talk at tinkleton church gate as the bell be toll in the sunshine and the white smock and scarlet cloak come troop over the green common to sunday worship in a hundred society round about--the word be that clavering park be to be inhabit again

the son of an outlaw live in a dismal old chateau near bruge this gentleman have make a feeble attempt to start in life with a commission in a dragoon regiment and have break down almost at the outset

before this time there have be not rumour unfavourable to lady clavering s reputation but unpleasant impression regard her ladyship
the good english people abroad be shy of make her acquaintance her manner be not the most refine her origin be lamentably low and doubtful

chapter xxiv a little innocent every house have its skeleton in it somewhere and it may be a comfort to some unhappy folk to think that the lucky and most wealthy of their neighbour have their misery and cause of disquiet
our little innocent muse of blanche who sing so nicely and talk so sweetly you would have think she must have make sunshine where ever she go be the skeleton or the misery or the bore or the nemesis of clavering house and of most of the inhabitant thereof
as one little stone in your own shoe or your horse s suffice to put either to torture and to make your journey miserable so in life a little obstacle be sufficient to obstruct your entire progress and subject you to endless annoyance and disquiet
who would have guess that such a smile little fairy as blanche amory could be the cause of discord in any family

she be a young lady of some genius exquisite sympathy and considerable literary attainment living like many another genius with relative who could not comprehend she
neither her mother nor her stepfather be person of a literary turn
bell s life and the racing calendar be the extent of the baronet s reading and lady clavering still write like a schoolgirl of thirteen and with an extraordinary disregard to grammar and spelling
and as miss amory feel very keenly that she be not appreciate and that she live with person who be not she equal in intellect or conversational power she lose no opportunity to acquaint her family circle with their inferiority to herself and not only be a martyr but take care to let everybody know that she be so
if she suffer as she say and think she did severely be we to wonder that a young creature of such delicate sensibility should shriek and cry out a good deal
without sympathy life be nothing and would it not have be a want of candour on her part to affect a cheerfulness which she do not feel or pretend a respect for those towards whom it be quite impossible she should entertain any reverence
if a poetess may not bemoan her lot of what earthly use be her lyre
blanche strike hers only to the sad of tune and sing elegy over her dead hope dirge over her early frost-nipt bud of affection as become such a melancholy fate and muse
her actual distress as we have say have not be up to the present time very considerable but her grief lay like those of most of we in her own soul--that be sad and habitually dissatisfied what wonder that she should weep
so mes larmes dribble out of her eye any day at command she could furnish an unlimited supply of tear and her faculty of shed they increase by practice
for sentiment be like another complaint mention by horace as increase by self-indulgence i be sorry to say lady that the complaint in question be call the dropsy and the more you cry the more you will be able and desirous to do so
missy have begin to gush at a very early age
lamartine be her favourite bard from the period when she first could feel and she have subsequently improve her mind by a sedulous study of novel of the great modern author of the french language
there be not a romance of balzac and george sand which the indefatigable little creature have not devour--by the time she be sixteen and however little she sympathise with her relative at home she have friend as she say in the spirit-world mean the tender indiana the passionate and poetic lelia the amiable trenmor that high-souled convict that angel of the galleys the fiery stenio and the other numberless hero of the french romance
she have be in love with prince rodolph and prince djalma while she be yet at school and have settle the divorce question and the right of woman with indiana before she have leave off pinafore

pretty little poetical spirit
it be curious to watch they with those plaything

as novelist be suppose to know everything even the secret of female heart which the owner themselves do not perhaps know we may state that at eleven year of age mademoiselle betsi as miss amory be then call have feel tender emotion towards a young savoyard organ-grinder at paris whom she persist in believe to be a prince carry off from his parent that at twelve an old and hideous drawing-master but ah what age or personal defect be proof against woman s love have agitate her young heart and that at thirteen be at madame de caramel s boarding-school in the champs elysees which as everybody know be next door to monsieur rogron s chevalier of the legion of honour pension for young gentleman a correspondence by letter take place between the seduisante miss betsi and two young gentleman of the college of charlemagne who be pensioner of the chevalier rogron
in the above paragraph our young friend have be call by a christian name different to that under which we be lately present to she
the fact be that miss amory call missy at home have really at the first be christen betsy--but assume the name of blanche of her own will and fantasy and crown herself with it and the weapon which the baronet her stepfather hold in terror over she be the threat to call she publicly by her name of betsy by which menace he sometimes manage to keep the young rebel in order
we have speak just now of child s doll and of the manner in which those little people take up and neglect their darling toy and very likely this history will show that miss blanche assume and put away her live doll with a similar girlish inconstancy
she have have host of dear dear darling friend ere now and have quite a little museum of lock of hair in her treasure-chest which she have gather in the course of her sentimental progress
some dear friend have marry some have go to other school one beloved sister she have lose from the pension and find again o horror
her darling her leocadie keep the book in her father s shop a grocer in the rue du bac in fact she have meet with a number of disappointment estrangement disillusionment as she call they in her pretty french jargon and have see and suffer a great deal for so young a woman
but it be the lot of sensibility to suffer and of confide tenderness to be deceive and she feel that she be only undergo the penalty of genius in these pang and disappointment of her young career
meanwhile she manage to make the honest lady her mother as uncomfortable as circumstance would permit and cause she worthy stepfather to wish she be dead
with the exception of captain strong whose invincible good-humour be proof against her sarcasm the little lady rule the whole house with he tongue
if lady clavering talk about sparrowgrass instead of asparagus or call an object a hobject as this unfortunate lady would sometimes do missy calmly correct she and frighten the good soul her mother into error only the more frequent as she grow more nervous under her daughter s eye
it be not to be suppose consider the vast interest which the arrival of the family at clavering park inspire in the inhabitant of the little town that madame fribsby alone of all the folk in clavering should have remain unmoved and incurious
at the first appearance of the park family in church madame note every article of toilette which the lady wear from their bonnet to their brodequin and take a survey of the attire of the lady maid in the pew allot to they
we fear that doctor portman s sermon though it be one of his old and most value composition have little effect upon madame fribsby on that day
in a very few day afterwards she have manage for herself an interview with lady clavering s confidential attendant in the housekeeper s room at the park and her card in french and english state that she receive the new fashion from paris from her correspondent madame victorine and that she be in the custom of make court and ball dress for the nobility and gentry of the shire be in the possession of lady clavering and miss amory and favourably receive as she be happy to hear by those lady
mrs
bonner lady clavering s lady become soon a great frequenter of madame fribsby s drawing-room and partook of many entertainment at the milliner s expense
a meal of green tea scandal hot sally-lunn cake and a little novel reading be always at the service of mrs
bonner whenever she be free to pass an evening in the town
and she find much more time for these pleasure than her junior officer miss amory s maid who seldom could be spar for a holiday and be work as hard as any factory-girl by that inexorable little muse her mistress
the muse love to be dress becomingly and have a lively fancy and a poetic desire for change be for alter her attire every day
she maid have a taste in dressmaking--to which art she have be an apprentice at paris before she enter into miss blanche s service there--be keep from morning till night alter and remodelle miss amory s habiliment and rise very early and go to bed very late in obedience to the untiring caprice of her little taskmistress
the girl be of respectable english parent
there be many of our people colonist of paris who have see well day who be not quite ruin who do not quite live upon charity and yet cannot get on without it and as her father be a cripple incapable of work and her return home would only increase the burthen and add to the misery of the family poor pincott be fain to stay where she could maintain herself and spare a little relief to her parent
our muse with the candour which distinguish she never fail to remind her attendant of the real state of matter
i should send you away pincott for you be a great deal too weak and your eye be fail you and you be always cry and snivel and want the doctor but i wish that your parent at home should be support and i go on endure you for their sake mind the dear blanche would say to her timid little attendant
or pincott your wretched appearance and slavish manner and red eye positively give i the migraine and i think i shall make you wear rouge so that you may look a little cheerful or pincott i cannot bear even for the sake of your starve parent that you should tear my hair out of my head in that manner and i will thank you to write to they and say that i dispense with your service after which sort of speech and after keep she for an hour tremble over her hair which the young lady love to have comb as she peruse one of her favourite french novel she would go to bed at one o clock and say pincott you may kiss i
good night
i should like you to have the pink dress ready for the morning and so with blessing upon her attendant she would turn round and go to sleep
the muse might lie in bed as long as she choose of a morning and avail herself of that privilege but pincott have to rise very early indeed to get her mistress s task do and have to appear next day with the same red eye and the same wan face which displease miss amory by their want of gaiety and cause the mistress to be so angry because the servant persist in be and look unwell and unhappy
not that blanche ever think she be a hard mistress
indeed she make quite a friend of pincott at time and write some very pretty verse about the lonely little tiring-maid whose heart be far away
our beloved blanche be a superior being and expect to be wait upon as such
and i do not know whether there be any other lady in this world who treat their servant or dependant so but it may be that there be such and that the tyranny which they exercise over their subordinate and the pang which they can manage to inflict with a soft voice and a well-breed simper be as cruel as those which a slave-driver administer with an oath and a whip
but blanche be a muse--a delicate little creature quite tremulous with excitability whose eye fill with tear at the small emotion and who know but that it be the very fineness of her feeling which cause they to be froisse so easily
you crush a butterfly by merely touch it
vulgar people have no idea of the sensibility of a muse
so little pincott be occupy all day and night in stitch hemming rip comb ironing crimping for her mistress read to she when in bed for the girl be mistress of the two language and have a sweet voice and manner--could take no share in madame fribsby s soiree nor indeed be she much miss or consider of sufficient consequence to appear at their entertainment
but there be another person connect with the clavering establishment who become a constant guest of our friend the milliner
this be the chief of the kitchen monsieur mirobolant with whom madame fribsby soon form an intimacy
not have be accustom to the appearance or society of person of the french nation the rustic inhabitant of clavering be not so favourably impress by monsieur alcide s manner and appearance as that gentleman might have desire that they should be
he walk among they quite unsuspiciously upon the afternoon of a summer day when his service be not require at the house in his usual favourite costume namely his light green frock or paletot his crimson velvet waistcoat with blue glass button his pantalon ecossais of a very large and decide check pattern his orange satin neckcloth and his jean-boot with tip of shiny leather these with a gold-embroider cap and a richly gilt cane or other variety of ornament of a similar tendency form his usual holiday costume in which he flatter himself there be nothing remarkable unless indeed the beauty of his person should attract observation and in which he consider that he exhibit the appearance of a gentleman of good parisian ton
he walk then down the street grin and ogle every woman he meet with glance which he mean should kill they outright and peer over the railing and in at the window where female be in the tranquil summer evening
but betsy mrs
pybus s maid shrink back with a lor bless we as alcide ogle she over the laurel-bush the miss bakers and their mamma star with wonder and presently a crowd begin to follow the interesting foreigner of ragged urchin and child who leave their dirt-pie in the street to pursue he
for some time he think that admiration be the cause which lead these person in his wake and walk on please himself that he could so easily confer on other so much harmless pleasure
but the little child and dirt-pie manufacturer be presently succeed by follower of a large growth and a number of lad and girl from the factory be let loose at this hour join the mob and begin laugh jeering hoot and call opprobrious name at the frenchman
some cry out frenchy
frenchy some exclaim frogs one ask for a lock of his hair which be long and in richly-flow ringlet and at length the poor artist begin to perceive that he be an object of derision rather than of respect to the rude grinning mob
it be at this juncture that madame fribsby spy the unlucky gentleman with the train at his heel and hear the scornful shout with which they assail he
she run out of her room and across the street to the persecute foreigner she hold out her hand and address he in his own language invite he into her abode and when she have house he fairly within her door she stand bravely at the threshold before the gibe factory girl and boy and say they be a pack of coward to insult a poor man who could not speak their language and be alone and without protection
the little crowd with some ironical cheer and hooting nevertheless feel the force of madame fribsby s vigorous allocution and retreat before she for the old lady be rather respected in the place and her oddity and her kindness have make she many friend there
poor mirobolant be grateful indeed to hear the language of his country ever so ill speak
frenchman pardon our fault in their language much more readily than we excuse their bad english and will face our blunder throughout a long conversation without the least propensity to grin
the rescue artist vow that madame fribsby be his guardian angel and that he have not as yet meet with such suavity and politeness among les anglaises
he be as courteous and complimentary to she as if it be the fairest and noblest of lady whom he be address for alcide mirobolant pay homage after his fashion to all womankind and never dream of a distinction of rank in the realm of beauty as his phrase be
a cream flavour with pineapple--a mayonnaise of lobster which he flatter himself be not unworthy of his hand or of she to whom he have the honour to offer it as an homage and a box of preserve fruit of provence be bring by one of the chef s aide-de-camp in a basket the next day to the milliner s and be accompany with a gallant note to the amiable madame fribsbi
her kindness alcide say have make a green place in the desert of his existence h suavity would ever contrast in memory with the grossierete of the rustic population who be not worthy to possess such a jewel an intimacy of the most confidential nature thus spring up between the milliner and the chief of the kitchen but i do not know whether it be with pleasure or mortification that madame receive the declaration of friendship which the young alcides proffer to she for he persist in call she la respectable fribsbi la vertueuse fribsbi and in state that he should consider she as his mother while he hop she would regard he as her son
ah
it be not very long ago fribsby think that word have be address to she in that dear french language indicate a different sort of attachment
and she sigh as she look up at the picture of her carabineer
for it be surprising how young some people s heart remain when their head have need of a front or a little hair-dye and at this moment madame fribsby as she tell young alcide feel as romantic as a girl of eighteen
when the conversation take this turn--and at their first intimacy madame fribsby be rather inclined so to lead it--alcide always politely diverge to another subject it be as his mother that he persist in consider the good milliner
he would recognise she in no other capacity and with that relationship the gentle lady be force to content herself when she find how deeply the artist s heart be engage elsewhere
he be not long before he describe to she the subject and origin of his passion
i declare myself to she say alcide lay his hand on his heart in a manner which be as novel as i be charm to think it be agreeable
where cannot love penetrate respectable madame fribsbi
cupid be the father of invention i inquire of the domestic what be the plat of which mademoiselle partook with most pleasure and build up my little battery accordingly
on a day when her parent have go to dine in the world and i be grieve to say that a grossi dinner at a restaurateur in the boulevard or in the palais royal seem to form the delight of these unrefined person the charming miss entertain some comrade of the pension and i advise myself to send up a little repast suitable to so delicate young palate
her lovely name be blanche
the name of the maiden be white the wreath of rose which she wear be white
i determine that my dinner should be as spotless as the snow
at her accustomed hour and instead of the rude gigot a l eau which be ordinarily serve at her too simple table i send she up a little potage a la reine--a la reine blanche i call it as white as her own tint-- and confection with the most fragrant cream and almond
i then offer up at her shrine a filet de merlan a l gnes and a delicate plat which i designate as eperlan a la sainte-therese and of which my charming miss partook with pleasure
i follow this by two little entree of sweetbread and chicken and the only brown thing which i permit myself in the entertainment be a little roast of lamb which i lay in a meadow of spinach surround with croustillon represent sheep and ornament with daisy and other savage flower
after this come my second service a pudding a la reine elizabeth who madame fribsbi know be a maiden princess a dish of opal-coloured plover s egg which i call nid de tourtereaux a la roucoule place in the midst of they two of those tender volatile bill each other and confection with butter a basket contain little gateaux of apricot which i know all young lady adore and a jelly of marasquin bland insinuating intoxicate as the glance of beauty

no--the man be odious but the woman--the woman
i own dear fribsbi be seducing

chapter xxv contain both love and jealousy our reader have already hear sir francis clavering s candid opinion of the lady who have give he her fortune and restore he to his native country and home and it must be own that the baronet be not far wrong in his estimate of his wife and that lady clavering be not the wise or the well educate of woman

some month afterwards amory make his appearance at calcutta have work his way out before the mast from the cape--marry the rich attorney s daughter in spite of that old speculator--set up as indigo-planter and fail--set up as agent and fail again--set up as editor of the sunderbund pilot and fail again--quarrel ceaselessly with his father-in-law and his wife during the progress of all these mercantile transaction and disaster and end his career finally with a crash which compel he to leave calcutta and go to new south wales

when the grand company begin to fill the house at clavering park the chevalier strong who as his patron say be never in the way or out of it seldom intrude himself upon its society but go elsewhere to seek his relaxation
ihave see plenty of grand dinner in my time he say and din by jove in a company where there be a king and royal duke at top and bottom and every man along the table have six star on his coat but dammy glanders this finery donot suit i and the english lady with their confound buckram air and the squire with their politic after dinner send i to sleep--sink i dead if they donot
i like a place where i can blow my cigar when the cloth be remove and when ibe thirsty have my beer in its native pewter so on a gala-day at clavering park the chevalier would content himself with superintend the arrangement of the table and drill the major-domo and servant and have look over the bill-of-fare with monsieur mirobolant would not care to take the least part in the banquet
send i up a cutlet and a bottle of claret to my room this philosopher would say and from the window of that apartment which command the terrace and avenue he would survey the company as they arrive in their carriage or take a peep at the lady in the hall through an oeil-de-boeuf which command it from his corridor
and the guest be seat strong would cross the park to captain glanders s cottage at clavering or to pay the landlady a visit at the clavering arms or to drop in upon madame fribsby over her novel and tea

where there be a public-house with a good tap of beer--where there be a tenant with a pretty daughter who play on the piano--to chatteris to the play or the barrack--to baymouth if any fun be on foot there to the rural fair or race the chevalier and his brown horse make their way continually and this worthy gentleman live at free quarter in a friendly country

while she and pen be whirl round as light and brisk as a couple of opera-dancer honest captain broadfoot and the lady round whose large waist he be cling be twist round very leisurely accord to their nature and indeed be in everybody s way

chapter xxix babylon our reader must now please to quit the wood and sea-shore of the west and the gossip of clavering and the humdrum life of poor little fairoak and transport himself with arthur pendennis on the alacrity coach to london whither he go once for all to face the world and to make his fortune
as the coach whirl through the night away from the friendly gate of home many a plan do the young man cast in his mind of future life and conduct prudence and peradventure success and fame
he know he be a well man than many who have hitherto be ahead of he in the race his first failure have cause he remorse and bring with it reflection it have not take away his courage or let we add his good opinion of himself
a hundred eager fancy and busy hope keep he awake
how much old his mishap and a year s thought and self-communion have make he than when twelve month since he pass on this road on his way to and from oxbridge
his thought turn in the night with inexpressible fondness and tenderness towards the fond mother who bless he when parting and who in spite of all his past fault and folly trust he and love he still
blessing be on she
he pray as he look up to the star overhead
o heaven

he feel he be not fit for such a mate as that as many a wild prodigal who have be pious and guiltless in early day keep away from a church which he use to frequent once--shun it but not hostile to it--only feel that he have no right in that pure place

chapter xxx the knights of the temple colleges school and inn of court still have some respect for antiquity and maintain a great number of the custom and institution of our ancestor with which those person who do not particularly regard their forefather or perhaps be not very well acquainted with they have long since do away
a well-ordain workhouse or prison si much well provide with the appliance of health comfort and cleanliness than a respectable foundation school a venerable college or a learn inn
in the latter place of residence man be content to sleep in dingy closet and to pay for the sitting-room and the cupboard which be their dormitory the price of a good villa and garden in the suburb or of a roomy house in the neglect square of the town
the poor mechanic in spitalfields have a cistern and an unbounded suppy of water at his command but the gentleman of the inn of court and the gentleman of the university have their supply of this cosmetic fetched in jug by laundress and bedmaker and live in abode which be erect long before the custom of cleanliness and decency obtain among we
there be individual still alive who sneer at the people and speak of they with epithet of scorn
gentleman there can be but little doubt that your ancestor be the great unwashed and in the temple especially it be pretty certain that only under the great difficulty and restriction the virtue which have be pronounce to be next to godliness could have be practise at all
old grump of the norfolk circuit who have live for more than thirty year in the chamber under those occupy by warrington and pendennis and who use to be awaken by the roaring of the shower-bath which those gentleman have erect in their apartment--a part of the content of which occasionally trickle through the roof into mr
grump s room declare that the practice be an absurd newfangle dandify folly and daily curse the laundress who slop the staircase by which he have to pass
grump now much more than half a century old have indeed never use the luxury in question
he have do without water very well and so have our father before he
of all those knight and baronet lord and gentleman bear arm whose escutcheon be paint upon the wall of the famous hall of the upper temple be there no philanthropist good-natured enough to devise a set of hummums for the benefit of the lawyer his fellow and successor
the temple historian make no mention of such a scheme
there be pump court and fountain court with their hydraulic apparatus but one never hear of a bencher disporting in the fountain and cannot but think how many a counsel learn in the law of old day might have benefit by the pump
nevertheless those venerable inns which have the lamb and flag and the winged horse for their ensign have attraction for person who inhabit they and a share of rough comfort and freedom which man always remember with pleasure
i donot know whether the student of law permit himself the refreshment of enthusiasm or indulge in poetical reminiscence as he pass by historical chamber and say yonder eldon live--upon this site coke muse upon littleton--here chitty toil--here barnewall and alderson join in their famous labour--here byles compose his great work upon bill and smith compile his immortal lead case--here gustavus still toil with solomon to aid he but the man of letter cannot but love the place which have be inhabit by so many of his brother or people by their creation as real to we at this day as the author whose child they be--and sir roger de coverley walk in the temple garden and discourse with mr
spectator about the beauty in hoop and patch who be saunter over the grass be just as lively a figure to i as old samuel johnson roll through the fog with the scotch gentleman at his heel on their way to dr
goldsmith s chamber in brick court or harry fielding with ink ruffle and a wet towel round his head dash off article at midnight for the covent garden journal while the printer s boy be asleep in the passage
if we could but get the history of a single day as it pass in any one of those four-story house in the dingy court where our friend pen and warrington dwelt some temple asmodeus might furnish we with a queer volume
there may be a great parliamentary counsel on the ground floor who drive off to belgravia at dinner-time when his clerk too become a gentleman and go away to entertain his friend and to take his pleasure
but a short time since he be hungry and briefless in some garret of the inn live by stealthy literature hop and wait and sicken and no client come exhaust his own mean and his friend kindness have to remonstrate humbly with dun and to implore the patience of poor creditor
ruin seem to be star he in the face when behold a turn of the wheel of fortune and the lucky wretch in possession of one of those prodigious prize which be sometimes draw in the great lottery of the bar
many a well lawyer than himself do not make a fifth part of the income of his clerk who a few month since could scarcely get credit for black for his master s unpaid boot
on the first floor perhaps you will have a venerable man whose name be famous who have live for half a century in the inn whose brain be full of book and whose shelf be store with classical and legal lore
he have live alone all these fifty year alone and for himself amass learn and compile a fortune
he come home now at night alone from the club where he have be din freely to the lonely chamber where he live a godless old recluse
when he die his inn will erect a tablet to his honour and his heir burn a part of his library
would you like to have such a prospect for your old age to store up learning and money and end so

on the other side of the third landing where pen and warrington live till long after midnight sit mr
paley who take the high honour and who be a fellow of his college who will sit and read and note case until two o clock in the morning who will rise at seven and be at the pleader s chamber as soon as they be open where he will work until an hour before dinner-time who will come home from hall and read and note case again until dawn next day when perhaps mr
arthur pendennis and his friend mr
warrington be return from some of their wild expedition
how differently employ mr
paley have be
he have not be throw himself away he have only be bring a great intellect laboriously down to the comprehension of a mean subject and in his fierce grasp of that resolutely exclude from his mind all high thought all well thing all the wisdom of philosopher and historian all the thought of poet all wit fancy reflection art love truth altogether--so that he may master that enormous legend of the law which he propose to gain his livelihood by expound
warrington and paley have be competitor for university honour in former day and have run each other hard and everybody say now that the former be waste his time and energy whilst all people praise paley for his industry
there may be doubt however as to which be use his time well
the one could afford time to think and the other never could
the one could have sympathy and do kindness and the other must need be always selfish
he could not cultivate a friendship or do a charity or admire a work of genius or kindle at the sight of beauty or the sound of a sweet song--he have no time and no eye for anything but his law-book
all be dark outside his reading-lamp
love and nature and art which be the expression of our praise and sense of the beautiful world of god be shut out from he
and as he turn off his lonely lamp at night he never think but that he have spend the day profitably and go to sleep alike thankless and remorseless
but he shudder when he meet his old companion warrington on the stair and shun he as one that be doom to perdition
it may have be the sight of that cadaverous ambition and self-complacent meanness which show itself in paley s yellow face and twinkle in his narrow eye or it may have be a natural appetite for pleasure and joviality of which it must be confess mr
pen be exceedingly fond which deter that luckless youth from pursue his design upon the bench or the woolsack with the ardour or rather steadiness which be requisite in gentleman who would climb to those seat of honour
he enjoy the temple life with a great deal of relish his worthy relative think he be read as become a regular student and his uncle write home congratulatory letter to the kind widow at fairoaks announce that the lad have sow his wild oat and be become quite steady
the truth be that it be a new sort of excitement to pen the life in which he be now engage and have give up some of the dandified pretension and fine-gentleman air which he have contract among his aristocratic college acquaintance of whom he now see but little the rough pleasure and amusement of a london bachelor be very novel and agreeable to he and he enjoy they all
time be he would have envy the dandy their fine horse in rotten row but he be content now to walk in the park and look at they
he be too young to succeed in london society without a well name and a large fortune than he have and too lazy to get on without these adjunct
old pendennis fondly think he be busy with law because he neglect the social advantage present to he and have be at half a dozen ball and evening party retreat before their dulness and sameness and whenever anybody make inquiry of the worthy major about his nephew the old gentleman say the young rascal be reform and could not be get away from his book

pen s real course of life and how much pleasure enter into his law study
a long morning s reading a walk in the park a pull on the river a stretch up the hill to hampstead and a modest tavern dinner a bachelor night pass here or there in joviality not vice for arthur pendennis admire woman so heartily that he never could bear the society of any of they that be not in his fancy at least good and pure a quiet evening at home alone with a friend and a pipe or two and a humble potation of british spirit whereof mrs
flanagan the laundress invariably test the quality these be our young gentleman s pursuit and it must be own that his life be not unpleasant
in term-time mr

indeed that hall of the upper temple be a sight not uninteresting and with the exception of some trifling improvement and anachronism which have be introduce into the practice there a man may sit down and fancy that he join in a meal of the seventeenth century
the bar have their mess the student their table apart the bencher sit at the high table on the raise platform surround by picture of judge of the law and portrait of royal personage who have honour its festivity with their presence and patronage
pen look about on his first introduction not a little amuse with the scene which he witness
among his comrade of the student class there be gentleman of all age from sixty to seventeen stout grey-head attorney who be proceed to take the superior dignity dandies and man--about town who wish for some reason to be barrister of seven year standing swarthy black-eyed native of the colonies who come to be call here before they practise in their own islands and many gentleman of the irish nation who make a sojourn in middle temple lane before they return to the green country of their birth
there be little squad of read student who talk law all dinner-time there be row man whose discourse be of scull match the red house vauxhall and the opera there be other great in politic and orator of the student debating club with all of which set except the first whose talk be an almost unknown and a quite unintereste language to he mr

the ancient and liberal inn of the upper temple provide in its hall and for a most moderate price an excellent wholesome dinner of soup meat tart and port wine or sherry for the barrister and student who attend that place of refection
the party be arrange in mess of four each of which quartet have its piece of beef or leg of mutton its sufficient apple-pie and its bottle of wine
but the honest habitue of the hall amongst the low rank of student who have a taste for good living have many harmless art by which they improve their banquet and innocent dodge if we may be permit to use an excellent phrase that have become vernacular since the appearance of the last dictionary by which they strive to attain for themselves more delicate food than the common every-day roast meat of the student table

he like to think he be consort with all sort of man--so he behold coalheaver in their tap-room boxer in their inn-parlour honest citizen disport in the suburb or on the river and he would have like to hob and nob with celebrate pickpocket or drink a pot of ale with a company of burglar and cracksman have chance afford he an opportunity of make the acquaintance of this class of society

they respect he as a high chap a fine fellow a regular young swell

if these position be make for we we acquiesce in they very easily and be always pretty ready to assume a superiority over those who be as good as ourselves
pen s condescension at this time of his life be a fine thing to witness
amongst man of ability this assumption and impertinence pass off with extreme youth but it be curious to watch the conceit of a generous and clever lad--there be something almost touch in that early exhibition of simplicity and folly so after read pretty hard of a morning and i fear not law merely but politic and general history and literature which be as necessary for the advancement and instruction of a young man as mere dry law after apply with tolerable assiduity to letter to review to elemental book of law and above all to the newspaper until the hour of dinner be draw nigh these young gentleman would sally out upon the town with great spirit and appetite and bent upon enjoy a merry night as they have pass a pleasant forenoon
it be a jovial time that of four-and-twenty when every muscle of mind and body be in healthy action when the world be new as yet and one move over it spur onwards by good spirit and the delightful capability to enjoy
if ever we feel young afterwards it be with the comrade of that time the tune we hum in our old age be those we learn then

the fielding s head have be a house of entertainment almost since the time when the famous author of tom jones preside as magistrate in the neighbouring bow street his place be point out and the chair say to have be his still occupy by the president of the night s entertainment
the worthy cutts the landlord of the fielding s head generally occupy this post when not disable by gout or other illness
his jolly appearance and fine voice may be remember by some of my male reader he use to sing profusely in the course of the harmonic meeting and his song be of what may be call the british brandy-and-water school of song--such as the good old english gentleman dear tom this brown jug and so forth--song in which pathos and hospitality be blend and the praise of good liquor and the social affection be chant in a baritone voice

he be one of the wittiest the most amiable and the most incorrigible of irishmen

chapter xxxiii which be pass in the neighbourhood of ludgate hill our imprisoned captain announce in smart and emphatic language in his prospectus that the time have come at last when it be necessary for the gentleman of england to band together in defence of their common right and their glorious order menace on all side by foreign revolution by intestine radicalism by the artful calumny of mill-owner and cotton-lord and the stupid hostility of the mass whom they gull and lead
the ancient monarchy be insult the captain say by a ferocious republican rabble
the church be desert by envious dissent and undermine by stealthy infidelity
the good institution which have make our country glorious and the name of english gentleman the proud in the world be leave without defence and expose to assault and contumely from man to whom no sanctuary be sacred for they believe in nothing holy no history venerable for they be too ignorant to have hear of the past and no law be bind which they be strong enough to break when their leader give the signal for plunder
it be because the king of france mistrust their gentleman mr
shandon remark that the monarchy of saint louis go down it be because the people of england still believe in their gentleman that this country encounter and overcome the great enemy a nation ever meet it be because we be head by gentleman that the eagles retreat before we from the donro to the garonne it be a gentleman who break the line at trafalgar and sweep the plain of waterloo bungay nod his head in a knowing manner and wink his eye when the captain come to the waterloo passage and warrington burst out laugh
you see how our venerable friend bungay be affect shandon say slily look up from his papers-- that s your true sort of test
i have use the duke of wellington and the battle of waterloo a hundred time and i never know the duke to fail the captain then go on to confess with much candour that up to the present time the gentleman of england confident of their right and careless of those who question it have leave the political interest of their order as they do the management of their estate or the settlement of their legal affair to person affect to each peculiar service and have permit their interest to be represent in the press by professional proctor and advocate
that time shandon profess to consider be now go by the gentleman of england must be their own champion the declare enemy of their order be brave strong numerous and uncompromising
they must meet their foe in the field they must not be belie and misrepresent by hireling advocate they must not have grub street publish gazette from whitehall thatbe a dig at bacon s people mr
bungay say shandon turn round to the publisher

warrington begin to laugh again have determine to bring forward a journal of which the principle be so-and-so
these man be proud of their order and anxious to uphold it cry out captain shandon flourish his paper with a grin
they be loyal to their sovereign by faithful conviction and ancestral allegiance they love their church where they would have their child worship and for which their forefather bleed they love their country and would keep it what the gentleman of england--yes the gentleman of england wewill have that in large cap bungay my boy have make it--the great and free in the world and as the name of some of they be append to the deed which secure our liberty at runnymede-- whatbe that ask mr

you be laugh the captain say to warrington about the obvious reason which i mention

we have say he go on that we cannot give the name of the party engage in this undertaking and that there be obvious reason for that concealment
we number influential friend in both houses of the senate and have secure ally in every diplomatic circle in europe
our source of intelligence be such as cannot by any possibility be make public--and indeed such as no other london or european journal could by any chance acquire
but this we be free to say that the very early information connect with the movement of english and continental politic will be find only in the column of the pall mall gazette the statesman and the capitalist the country gentleman and the divine will be amongst our reader because our writer be amongst they
we address ourselves to the high circle of society we care not to disown it--the pall mall gazette be write by gentleman for gentleman its conductor speak to the class in which they live and be bear
the field-preacher have his journal the radical free-thinker have his journal why should the gentlemen of england be unrepresented in the press mr
shandon then go on with much modesty to descant upon the literary and fashionable department of the pall mall gazette which be to be conduct by gentleman of acknowledge reputation man famous at the universities at which mr pendennis could scarcely help laugh and blushing know at the clubs and of the society which they describe
he point out delicately to advertiser that there would be no such medium as the pall mall gazette for give publicity to their sale and he eloquently call upon the nobility of england the baronetage of england the revered clergy of england the bar of england the matron the daughter the home and hearth of england to rally round the good old cause and bungay at the conclusion of the reading wake up from a second snooze in which he have indulge himself and again say it be all right

i never know an embarrassed irish gentleman yet but he have an aide-de-camp of his own nation likewise in circumstance of pecuniary discomfort

it be hard to see such a man as shandon pen say musing and talk that night over the sight which he have witness of accomplishment so multifarious and of such an undoubted talent and humour an inmate of a gaol for half his time and a bookseller s hanger-on when out of prison i be a bookseller s hanger-on--you be go to try your pace as a hack warrington say with a laugh
we be all hack upon some road or other

bravo pen my be boy warrington answer laugh still
what have you get to say against bungay s relation with shandon
be it the publisher think you who send the author to prison
be it bungay who be tipple away the five-pound note which we see just now or shandon misfortune drive a man into bad company pen say
it be easy to cry fie against a poor fellow who have no society but such as he find in a prison and no resource except forgetfulness and the bottle
we must deal kindly with the eccentricity of genius and remember that the very ardour and enthusiasm of temperament which make the author delightful often lead the man astray a fiddlestick about man of genius warrington cry out who be a very severe moralist upon some point though possibly a very had practitioner
i deny that there be so many genius as people who whimper about the fate of man of letter assert there be
there be thousand of clever fellow in the world who could if they would turn verse write article read book and deliver a judgment upon they the talk of professional critic and writer be not a whit more brilliant or profound or amusing than that of any other society of educated people
if a lawyer or a soldier or a parson outrun his income and do not pay his bill he must go to gaol and an author must go too
if an author fuddle himself i donot know why he should be let off a headache the next morning if he order a coat from the tailor s why he shouldnot pay for it i would give he more money to buy coat say pen smile
i suppose i should like to belong to a well-dress profession
i protest against that wretch of a middle-man whom i see between genius and his great landlord the public and who stop more than half of the labourer s earning and fame i be a prose labourer warrington say you my boy be a poet in a small way and so i suppose consider you be authorise to be flighty
what be it you want
do you want a body of capitalist that shall be force to purchase the work of all author who may present themselves manuscript in hand
everybody who write his epic every driveller who can or cannot spell and produce his novel or his tragedy are they all to come and find a bag of sovereign in exchange for their worthless ream of paper
who be to settle what be good or bad saleable or otherwise
will you give the buyer leave in fine to purchase or not
why sir when johnson sate behind the screen at saint john s gate and take his dinner apart because he be too shabby and poor to join the literary bigwig who be regale themselves round mr
cave s good table-cloth the tradesman be do he no wrong

it have a right to deal with the literary inventor as with any other if i produce a novelty in the book trade i must do the good i can with it but i can no more force mr
murray to purchase my book of travel or sermon than i can compel mr
tattersall to give i a hundred guineas for my horse
i may have my own idea of the value of my pegasus and think he the most wonderful of animal but the dealer have a right to his opinion too and may want a lady s horse or a cob for a heavy timid rider or a sound hack for the road and my beast willnot suit he you deal in metaphor warrington pen say but you rightly say that you be very prosaic
poor shandon
there be something about the kindness of that man and the gentleness of that sweet creature of a wife which touch i profoundly
i like he i be afraid well than a well man and so do i warrington say

be it true think pendennis lie on his bed and gaze at a bright moon without that light up a corner of his dressing-table and the frame of a little sketch of fairoak draw by laura and hang over his drawer--be it true that i be go to earn my bread at last and with my pen
that i shall impoverish the dear mother no long and that i may gain a name and reputation in the world perhaps
these be welcome if they come think the young visionary laugh and blush to himself though alone and in the night as he think how dearly he would relish honour and fame if they could be his
if fortune favour i i laud she if she frown i resign she
i pray heaven i may be honest if i fail or if i succeed
i pray heaven i may tell the truth as far as i know it that i maynot swerve from it through flattery or interest or personal enmity or party prejudice

he go on with these musing more happy and hopeful and in a humbler frame of mind than he have feel to be for many a day
he think over the error and idleness the passion extravagance disappointment of his wayward youth he get up from the bed throw open the window and look out into the night and then by some impulse which we hope be a good one he go up and kiss the picture of fairoaks and fling himself down on his knee by the bed remain for some time in that posture of hope and submission
when he rise it be with streaming eye

chapter xxxiv in which the history still hover about fleet street captain shandon urge on by his wife who seldom meddle in business matter have stipulate that john finucane esquire of the upper temple should be appoint sub-editor of forthcoming pall mall gazette and this post be accordingly confer upon mr
finucane by the spirited proprietor of the journal

chapter xxxvi the pall mall gazette considerable success at first attend the new journal
it be generally state that an influential political party support the paper and great name be cite amongst the contributor to its column
be there any foundation for these rumour
we be not at liberty to say whether they be ill-found but this much we may divulge that an article upon foreign policy which be generally attribute to a noble lord whose connexion with the foreign office be very well know be in reality compose by captain shandon in the parlour of the bear and staff public-house near whitehall stairs whither the printer s boy have track he and where a literary ally of his mr

george warrington of the upper temple

the pall mall gazette have its office as we have hear in catherine street in the strand and hither pen often come with his manuscript in his pocket and with a great deal of bustle and pleasure such as a man feel at the outset of his literary career when to see himself in print be still a novel sensation and he yet please himself to think that his writing be create some noise in the world
here it be that mr
jack finucane the sub-editor compile with paste and scissor the journal of which he be supervisor
with an eagle eye he scan all the paragraph of all the newspaper which have anything to do with the world of fashion over which he preside
he donot let a death or a dinner-party of the aristocracy pass without have the event record in the column of his journal and from the most recondite provincial print and distant scotch and irish newspaper he fish out astonishing paragraph and intelligence regard the upper class of society
it be a grand nay a touching sight for a philosopher to see jack finucane esquire with a plate of meat from the cookshop and glass of porter from the public-house for his meal recount the feast of the great as if h have be present at they and in tattered trouser and dingy shirt-sleeve cheerfully describe and arrange the most brilliant fete of the world of fashion
the incongruity of finucane s avocation and his manner and appearance amuse his new friend pen
since he leave his own native village where his rank probably be not very lofty jack have seldom see any society but such as use the parlour of the tavern which he frequent whereas from his writing you would have suppose that he din with ambassador and that his common lounge be the bow-window of white s
error of description it be true occasionally slip from his pen but the ballinafad sentinel of which he be own correspondent suffer by these not the pall mall gazette in which jack be not permit to write much his london chief think that the scissor and the paste be well wield by he than the pen
pen take a great deal of pain with the writing of his review and have a pretty fair share of desultory reading acquire in the early year of his life an eager fancy and a keen sense of fun his article please his chief and the public and he be proud to think that he deserve the money which he earn
we may be sure that the pall mall gazette be take in regularly at fairoaks and read with delight by the two lady there

and what be the astonishment and delight of our friend major pendennis on walk into one of his club the regent where wenham lord falconet and some other gentleman of good reputation and fashion be assemble to hear they one day talk over a number of the pall mall gazette and of an article which appear in its column make some bitter fun of the book recently publish by the wife of a celebrated member of the opposition party
the book in question be a book of travels in spain and italy by the countess of muffborough in which it be difficult to say which be the most wonderful the french or the english in which language her ladyship write indifferently and upon the blunder of which the critic pounce with delightful mischief
the critic be no other than pen he jump and dance round about his subject with the great jocularity and high spirit he show up the noble lady s fault with admirable mock gravity and decorum
there be not a word in the article which be not polite and gentlemanlike and the unfortunate subject of the criticism be scarify and laugh at during the operation
wenham s bilious countenance be pucker up with malign pleasure as he read the critique
lady muffborough have not ask he to her party during the last year
lord falconet giggle and laugh with all his heart lord muffborough and he have be rival ever since they begin life and these compliment major pendennis who until now have scarcely pay any attention to some hint which his fairoaks correspondence throw out of dear arthur s constant and severe literary occupation which i fear may undermine the poor boy s health and have think any notice of mr

pen fling his letter across the table to warrington perhaps he be disappoint that the other do not seem to be much affect by it
the courage of young critic be prodigious they clamber up to the judgment-seat and with scarce a hesitation give their opinion upon work the most intricate or profound
have macaulay s history or herschel s astronomy be put before pen at this period he would have look through the volume meditate his opinion over a cigar and signify his august approval of either author as if the critic have be their bear superior and indulgent master and patron
by the help of the biographie universelle or the british museum he would be able to take a rapid resume of a historical period and allude to name date and fact in such a masterly easy way as to astonish his mamma at home who wonder where her boy could have acquire such a prodigious store of reading and himself too when he come to read over his article two or three month after they have be compose and when he have forget the subject and the book which he have consult

pen own that he would not have hesitate at twenty-four hour notice to pass his opinion upon the great scholar or to give a judgment upon the encyclopaedia
luckily he have warrington to laugh at he and to keep down his impertinence by a constant and wholesome ridicule or he might have become conceited beyond all sufferance for shandon like the dash and flippancy of his young aide-de-camp and be indeed well pleased with pen s light and brilliant flash than with the heavy metal which his eld coadjutor bring to bear
but though he might justly be blame on the score of impertinence and a certain prematurity of judgment mr

chapter xxxvii where pen appear in town and country let we be allow to pass over a few month of the history of mr
arthur pendennis s lifetime during the which many event may have occur which be more interesting and exciting to himself than they would be likely to prove to the reader of his present memoir
we leave he in his last chapter regularly enter upon his business as a professional writer or literary hack as mr
warrington choose to style himself and his friend and we know how the life of any hack legal or literary in a curacy or in a march regiment or at a merchant s desk be dull of routine and tedious of description
one day s labour resemble another much too closely
a literary man have often to work for his bread against time or against his will or in spite of his health or of his indolence or of his repugnance to the subject on which he be call to exert himself just like any other daily toiler
when you want to make money by pegasus as he must perhaps who have no other saleable property farewell poetry and aerial flight pegasus only rise now like mr
green s balloon at period advertise beforehand and when the spectator s money have be pay
pegasus trot in harness over the stony pavement and pull a cart or a cab behind he
often pegasus do his work with pant side and tremble knee and not seldom get a cut of the whip from his driver
do not let we however be too prodigal of our pity upon pegasus
there be no reason why this animal should be exempt from labour or illness or decay any more than any of the other creature of god s world
if he get the whip pegasus often deserve it and i for one be quite ready to protest my friend george warrington against the doctrine which poetical sympathiser be inclined to put forward viz that of letter and what be call genius be to be exempt from prose duty of this daily bread-wanting tax-pay life and not to be make to work and pay like their neighbour
well then the pall mall gazette be duly establish and arthur pendennis s merit recognise as a flippant witty and amusing critic he work away hard every week prepare review of such work as come into his department and write his review with flippancy certainly but with honesty and to the good of his power
it might be that a historian of threescore who have spend a quarter of a century in compose a work of which our young gentleman dispose in the course of a couple of day reading at the british museum be not altogether fairly treat by such a facile critic or that a poet who have be elaborate sublime sonnet and ode until he think they fit for the public and for fame be annoy by two or three dozen pert line in mr
pen s review in which the poet s claim be settle by the critic as if the latter be my lord on the bench and the author a miserable little suitor tremble before he
the actor at the theatre complain of he wofully too and very likely he be too hard upon they
but there be not much harm do after all
it be different now as we know but there be so few great historian or great poet or great actor in pen s time that scarce any at all come up for judgment his critical desk
those who get a little whipping get what in the main be good for they not that the judge be any well or wise than the person whom he sentence or indeed ever fancy himself so
pen have a strong sense of humour and justice and have not therefore an overweening respect for his own work besides he have his friend warrington at his elbow--a terrible critic if the young man be dispose to be conceite and more savage over pen than ever he be to those whom he try at his literary assize
by these critical labour and by occasional contribution to lead article of the journal when without wound his paper this eminent publicist could conscientiously speak his mind mr
arthur pendennis gain the sum of four pound four shilling weekly and with no small pain and labour
likewise be furnish magazines and reviews with article of his composition and be believe to have be though on this score he never choose to speak london correspondent of the chatteris champion which at that time contain some very brilliant and eloquent letter from the metropolis
by these labour the fortunate youth be enable to earn a sum very nearly equal to four hundred pound a year and on the second christmas after his arrival in london he actually bring a hundred pound to his mother as a dividend upon the debt which he owe to laura
that mrs
pendennis read every word of her son s work and consider he to be the profound thinker and most elegant writer of the day that she think his retribution of the hundred pound an act of angelic virtue that she fear he be ruin his health by his labour and be delighted when he tell she of the society which he meet and of the great man of letter and fashion whom he see will be imagine by all reader who have see son-worship amongst mother and that charming simplicity of love with which woman in the country watch the career of their darling in london
if john have hold such and such a brief if tom have be invite to such and such a ball or george have meet this or that great and famous man at dinner what a delight there be in the heart of mother and sister at home in somersetshire
how young hopeful s letter be read and remember
what a theme for village talk they give and friendly congratulation
in the second winter pen come for a very brief space and cheer the widow s heart and lighten up the lonely house at fairoaks

pen and treat he with marked respect between mother and son it be all fondness confidence and affection
it be the happy fortnight of the widow s whole life perhaps in the life of both of they
the holiday be go only too quickly and pen be back in the busy world and the gentle widow alone again
she send arthur s money to laura i donot know why this young lady take the opportunity of leave home when pen be come thither or whether he be the more pique or relieve by her absence
he be by this time by his own merit and his uncle s introduction pretty well introduce into london and know both in literary and polite circle
amongst the former his fashionable reputation stand he in no little stead he be consider to be a gentleman of good present mean and well expectation who write for his pleasure than which there cannot be a great recommendation to a young literary aspirant
bacon bungay and co

wenham ask he to dinner mr
wagg look upon he with a favourable eye and they report how they meet he at the house of person of fashion amongst whom he be pretty welcome as they do not trouble themselves about his mean present or future as his appearance and address be good and as he have get a character for be a clever fellow
finally he be ask to one house because he be see at another house and thus no small variety of london life be present to the young man he be make familiar with all sort of people from paternoster row to pimlico and be as much at home at mayfair dining-table as at those tavern board where some of his companion of the pen be accustom to assemble
full of high spirit and curiosity easily adapt himself to all whom he meet the young fellow please himself in this strange variety and jumble of man and make himself welcome or at ease at least wherever he go
he would breakfast for instance at mr
plover s of a morning in company with a peer a bishop a parliamentary orator two blue lady of fashion a popular preacher the author of the last new novel and the very late lion import from egypt or from america and would quit this distinguished society for the back room at the newspaper office where pen and ink and the wet proof-sheet be await he
here would be finucane the sub-editor with the last news from the row and shandon would come in presently and give a nod to pen would begin scribble his lead article at the other end of the table flank by the pint of sherry which when the attendant boy behold he be always silently bring for the captain or mr

midge the publisher and after look through the volume would sell they at his accustomed bookstall and have drunken and din upon the produce of the sale in a tavern box would call for ink and paper and proceed to smash the author of his dinner and the novel
towards evening mr
pen would stroll in the direction of his club and take up warrington there for a constitutional walk
this exercise free the lung and give an appetite for dinner after which pen have the privilege to make his bow at some very pleasant house which be open to he or the town before he for amusement
there be the opera or the eagle tavern or a ball to go to in mayfair or a quiet night with a cigar and a book and a long talk with warrington or a wonderful new song at the back kitchen at this time of his life mr
pen beheld all sort of place and man and very likely do not know how much he enjoy himself until long after when ball give he no pleasure neither do farce make he laugh nor do the tavern joke produce the least excitement in he nor do the lovely dancer that ever show her ankle cause he to stir from his chair after dinner
at his present mature age all these pleasure be over and the time have pass away too
it be but a very very few year since--but the time be go and most of the man
bludyer will no more bully author or cheat landlord of their score
shandon the learn and thriftless the witty and unwise sleep his last sleep
they bury honest doolan the other day never will he cringe or flatter never pull long-bow or empty whisky-noggin any more
the london season be now bloom in its full vigour and the fashionable newspaper abound with information regard the grand banquet rout and ball which be enliven the polite world
our gracious sovereign be hold levee and drawing-room at st
james s the bow-window of the club be crowd with the head of respectable red-faced newspaper-read gentleman along the serpentine trail thousand of carriage squadron of dandy horseman trample over rotten row everybody be in town in a word and of course major arthur pendennis who be somebody be not absent
with his head tie up in a smart bandana handkerchief and his meagre carcass envelop in a brilliant turkish dressing-gown the worthy gentleman sate on a certain morning by his fireside let his foot gently simmer in a bath whilst he take his early cup of tea and peruse his morning post
he could not have face the day without his two hour toilet without his early cup of tea without his morning post
i suppose nobody in the world except morgan not even morgan s master himself know how feeble and ancient the major be grow and what numberless little comfort he require
if man sneer as our habit be at the artifice of an old beauty at her paint perfume ringlet at those innumerable and to we unknown stratagem with which she be say to remedy the ravage of time and reconstruct the charm whereof year have bereft she the lady it be to be presume be not on their side altogether ignorant that man be vain as well as they and that the toilet of old buck be to the full as elaborate as their own
how be it that old blushington keep that constant little rose-tint on his cheek and where do old blondel get the preparation which make his silver hair pass for golden
have you ever see lord hotspur get off his horse when he think nobody be look
take out of his stirrup his shiny boot can hardly totter up the step of hotspur house
he be a dash young nobleman still as you see the back of he in rotten row when you behold he on foot what an old old fellow
do you ever form to yourself any idea of dick lacy dick have be dick these sixty year in a natural state and without his stay
all these man be object whom the observer of human life and manner may contemplate with as much profit as the most elderly belgravian venus or inveterate mayfair jezebel
an old reprobate daddy-longleg who have never say his prayer except perhaps in public these fifty year an old buck who still cling to as many of the habit of youth as his feeble grasp of health can hold by who have give up the bottle but sit with young fellow over it and tell naughty story upon toast-and-water--who have give up beauty but still talk about it as wickedly as the young roue in company--such an old fellow i say if any parson in pimlico or st
james s be to order the beadle to bring he into the middle aisle and there set he in an armchair and make a text of he and preach about he to the congregation could be turn to a wholesome use for once in his life and might be surprised to find that some good thought come out of he
but we be wander from our text the honest major who sit all this while with his foot cool in the bath morgan take they out of that place of purification and dry they daintily and proceed to set the old gentleman on his leg with waistband and wig starched cravat and spotless boot and glove
it be during these hour of the toilet that morgan and his employer have their confidential conversation for they do not meet much at other time of the day--the major abhor the society of his own chair and table in his lodging and morgan his master s toilet over and letter deliver have his time very much on his own hand
this spare time the active and well-mannered gentleman bestow among the valet and butler of the nobility his acquaintance and morgan pendennis as he be style for by such compound name gentleman s gentleman be call in their private circle be a frequent and welcome guest at some of the very high table in this town
he be a member of two influential club in mayfair and pimlico and he be thus enable to know the whole gossip of the town and entertain his master very agreeably during the two hour toilet conversation
he know a hundred tale and legend regard person of the very high ton whose valet canvass their august secret just my dear madam as our own parlour-maid and dependant in the kitchen discuss our character our stinginess and generosity our pecuniary mean or embarrassment and our little domestic or connubial tiff and quarrel
if i leave this manuscript open on my table i have not the slight doubt betty will read it and they will talk it over in the low region to-night and to-morrow she will bring in my breakfast with a face of such entire imperturbable innocence that no mortal could suppose she guilty of play the spy
if you and the captain have high word upon any subject which be just possible the circumstance of the quarrel and the character of both of you will be discuss with impartial eloquence over the kitchen tea-table and if mrs
smith s maid should by chance be take a dish of tea with your her presence will not undoubtedly act as a restraint upon the discussion in question her opinion will be give with candour and the next day her mistress will probably know that captain and mrs
jones have be a quarrelling as usual
nothing be secret
take it as a rule that john know everything and as in our humble world so in the great a duke be no more a hero to his valet-de-chambre than you or i and his grace s man at his club in company doubtless with other men of equal social rank talk over his master s character and affair with the ingenuous truthfulness which befit gentleman who be meet together in confidence
who be a niggard and screw up his money-box who be in the hand of the money-lender and be put his noble name on the back of bill of exchange who be intimate with whose wife who want whom to marry her daughter and which he willnot no not at any price all these fact gentleman s confidential gentleman discuss confidentially and be know and examine by every person who have any claim to rank in genteel society
in a word if old pendennis himself be say to know everything and be at once admirably scandalous and delightfully discreet it be but justice to morgan to say that a great deal of his master s information be supply to that worthy man by his valet who go out and forage knowledge for he
indeed what more effectual plan be there to get a knowledge of london society than to begin at the foundation--that be at the kitchen floor
so mr
morgan and his employer converse as the latter s toilet proceed
there have be a drawing-room on the previous day and the major read among the presentation that of lady clavering by lady rockminster and of miss amory by her mother lady clavering and in a further part of the paper their dress be describe with a precision and in a jargon which will puzzle and amuse the antiquary of future generation
the sight of these name carry pendennis back to the country

it be a known fact that an envoy from an indian prince a colonel altamont the nawaub of lucknow s prime favourite an extraordinary man who have it be say embrace mahometanism and undergo a thousand wild and perilous adventure be at present in this country try to negotiate with the begum clavering the sale of the nawaub s celebrated nose-ring diamond the light of the dewan under the title of the begum lady clavering s fame begin to spread in london before she herself descend upon the capital and as it have be the boast of delolme and blackstone and all panegyrist of the british constitution that we admit into our aristocracy merit of every kind and that the lowliest-bear man if he but deserve it may wear the robe of a peer and sit alongside of a cavendish or a stanley so it ought to be the boast of our good society that haughty though it be naturally jealous of its privilege and careful who shall be admit into its circle yet if an individual be but rich enough all barrier be instantly remove and he or she be welcome as from his wealth he merit to be
this fact show our british independence and honest feeling--our high order be not such mere haughty aristocrat as the ignorant represent they on the contrary if a man have money they will hold out their hand to he eat his dinner dance at his ball marry his daughter or give their own lovely girl to his son as affably as your commonest roturier would do

pen during his residence at home after his defeat at oxbridge have occupy himself with various literary composition and amongst other work have write the great part of a novel
this book write under the influence of his youthful embarrassment amatory and pecuniary be of a very fierce gloomy and passionate sort the byronic despair the wertherian despondency the mocking bitterness of mephistopheles of faust be all reproduce and develop in the character of the hero for our youth have just be learn the german language and imitate as almost all clever lad do his favourite poet and writer
passage in the volume once so love and now read so seldom still bear the mark of the pencil with which he note they in those day
tear fall upon the leaf of the book perhaps or blister the page of his manuscript as the passionate young man dash his thought down
if he take up the book afterwards he have no ability or wish to sprinkle the leaf with that early dew of former time his pencil be no long eager to score its mark of approval but as he look over the page of his manuscript he remember what have be overflow feeling which have cause he to blot it and the pain which have inspire the line
if the secret history of book could be write and the author s private thought and meaning note down alongside of his story how many insipid volume would become interesting and dull tale excite the reader
many a bitter smile pass over pen s face as he read his novel and recall the time and feeling which give it birth
how pompous some of the grand passage appear and how weak be other in which he think he have express his full heart
this page be imitate from a then favourite author as he could now clearly see and confess though he have believe himself to be write originally then

and what mean those blot on the page

now though he have keep it in his box for nearly half of the period during which accord to the horatian maxim a work of art ought to lie ripen a maxim the truth of which may by the way be question altogether mr
pen have not bury his novel for this time in order that the work might improve but because he do not know where else to bestow it or have no particular desire to see it
a man who think of put away a composition for ten year before he shall give it to the world or exercise his own mature judgment upon it have well be very sure of the original strength and durability of the work otherwise on withdraw it from its crypt he may find that like small wine it have lose what flavour it once have and be only tasteless when open
there be work of all taste and smack the small and the strong those that improve by age and those that willnot bear keep at all but be pleasant at the first draught when they refresh and sparkle
now pen have never any notion even in the time of his youthful inexperience and fervour of imagination that the story he be write be a masterpiece of composition or that he be the equal of the great author whom he admire and when he now review his little performance he be keenly enough alive to its fault and pretty modest regard its merit
it be not very good he think but it be as good as most book of the kind that have the run of circulate library and the career of the season

let no young people be mislead and rush fatally into romance-writing for one book which succeed let they remember the many that fail i do not say deservedly or otherwise and wholesomely abstain or if they venture at least let they do so at their own peril
