when at length the famous novel appear it not only meet with applause from more impartial critic than mrs
pendennis but luckily for pen it suit the taste of the public and obtain a quick and considerable popularity before two month be over pen have the satisfaction and surprise of see the second edition of walter lorraine advertise in the newspaper and enjoy the pleasure of read and send home the critique of various literary journal and reviewer upon his book
their censure do not much affect he for the good-natured young man be dispose to accept with considerable humility the dispraise of other
nor do their praise elate he over much for like most honest person he have his own opinion about his own performance and when a critic praise he in the wrong place he be rather hurt than pleased by the compliment
but if a review of his work be very laudatory it be a great pleasure to he to send it home to his mother at fairoaks and to think of the joy which it would give there
there be some nature and perhaps as we have say pendennis s be one which be improve and soften by prosperity and kindness as there be man of other disposition who become arrogant and graceless under good fortune
happy he who can endure one or the other with modesty and good-humour
lucky he who have be educate to bear his fate whatsoever it may be by an early example of uprightness and a childish training in honour
chapter xliii alsatia breed up like a bailiff or a shabby attorney about the purlieus of the inns of court shepherd s inn be always to be find in the close neighbourhood of lincoln s-inn fields and the temple
some where behind the black gable and smutty chimney-stack of wych street holywell street chancery lane the quadrangle lie hide from the outer world and it be approach by curious passage and ambiguous smoky alley on which the sun have forget to shine
slop-seller brandy-ball and hard-bake vendor purveyor of theatrical print for youth dealer in dingy furniture and bedding suggestive of anything but sleep line the narrow wall and dark casement with their ware
the door be many-bell and crowd of dirty child form endless group about the step or around the shell-fish dealer tray in these court whereof the damp pavement resound with patten and be drabble with a never-fail mud
ballad-singer come and chant here in deadly guttural tone satirical song against the whig administration against the bishop and dignified clergy against the german relative of an august royal family punch set up his theatre sure of an audience and occasionally of a halfpenny from the swarm occupant of the house woman scream after their child for loiter in the gutter or bad still against the husband who come reel from the gin-shop there be a ceaseless din and life in these court out of which you pass into the tranquil old-fashioned quadrangle of shepherd s inn
in a mangy little grass-plat in the centre rise up the statue of shepherd defend by iron railing from the assault of boy
the hall of the inn on which the founder s arm be paint occupy one side of the square the tall and ancient chamber be carry round other two side and over the central archway which lead into oldcastle street and so into the great london thoroughfare
the inn may have be occupy by lawyer once but the laity have long since be admit into its precinct and i do not know that any of the principal legal firm have their chamber here
the office of the polwheedle and tredyddlum copper mines occupy one set of the ground-floor chamber the registry of patent inventions and union of genius and capital company another the only gentleman whose name figure here and in the law list be mr
campion who wear mustachio and who come in his cab twice or thrice in a week and whose west end office be in curzon street mayfair where mrs
campion entertain the nobility and gentry to whom her husband lend money

somerset campion here he be campion and co and the same tuft which ornament his chin sprout from the under lip of the rest of the firm

the rein and the breech of the groom be glitter white the lustre of that equipage make a sunshine in that shady place

on one day a bailiff be about to lead he to prison he write unless the--to you insignificant--sum of three pound five can be forthcoming to liberate a poor man s grey hair from gaol and the good-natured lady mirabel despatch the money necessary for her father s liberation with a caution to he to be more economical for the future
on a second occasion the captain meet with a frightful accident and break a plate-glass window in the strand for which the proprietor of the shop hold he liable

a strange and motley set they be these friend of the chevalier and though major pendennis would not much have relish their company arthur and warrington like it not a little and pen think it as amusing as the society of the fine gentleman in the fine house which he have the honour to frequent

filby have be everything a corporal of dragoon a field-preacher and missionary-agent for convert the irish an actor at a greenwich fair-booth in front of which his father s attorney find he when the old gentleman die and leave he that famous property from which he get no rent now and of which nobody exactly know the situation
add to these be sir francis clavering bart who like their society though he do not much add to its amusement by his convivial power
but he be make much of by the company now on account of his wealth and position in the world
he tell his little story and sing his little song or two with great affability and he have have his own history too before his accession to good fortune and have see the inside of more prison than one and write his name on many a stamp paper
when altamont first return from paris and after he have communicate with sir francis clavering from the hotel at which he have take up his quarter and which he have reach in a very denude state consider the wealth of diamond and ruby with which this honest man be entrust strong be send to his patron by the baronet pay his little bill at the inn and invite he to come and sleep for a night or two at the chamber where he subsequently take up his residence

chapter xliv in which the colonel narrate some of his adventure early in the forenoon of the day after the dinner in grosvenor place at which colonel altamont have choose to appear the colonel emerge from his chamber in the upper story at shepherd s inn and enter into strong s sitting-room where the chevalier sate in his easy-chair with the newspaper and his cigar

that enjoyment never fail he he have always an easy temper a faultless digestion and a rosy cheek and whether he be go into action the next morning or to prison and both have be his lot in the camp or the fleet the worthy captain snore healthfully through the night and wake with a good heart and appetite for the struggle or difficulty or pleasure of the day

he advance towards that fane in a carriage-and-four so to speak and might halt and take refreshment almost whenever he please

young frank clavering steal his father s havannahs and convey they to school or smoke they in the stable at a surprisingly early period of life and at ten year old drink his champagne almost as stoutly as any whiskered cornet of dragoon could do

chapter xlvii monseigneur s amuse the exertion of that last night at gaunt house have prove almost too much for major pendennis and as soon as he could move his weary old body with safety he transport himself groan to buxton and seek relief in the healing water of that place
parliament break up

the town be however empty since then

warrington stow away a quantity of cavendish tobacco in his carpet-bag and betook himself as his custom be in the vacation to his brother s house in norfolk
pen be leave alone in chamber for a while for this man of fashion could not quit the metropolis when he choose always and be at present detain by the affair of his newspaper the pall mall gazette of which he act as the editor and charge d affaires during the temporary absence of the chief captain shandon who be with his family at the salutary watering-place of boulogne-sur-mer
although as we have see mr
pen have pronounce himself for year past to be a man perfectly blase and weary of life yet the truth be that he be an exceedingly healthy young fellow still with a fine appetite which he satisfy with the great relish and satisfaction at least once a day and a constant desire for society which show he to be anything but misanthropical
if he could not get a good dinner he sate down to a bad one with perfect contentment if he could not procure the company of witty or great or beautiful person he put up with any society that come to hand and be perfectly satisfied in a tavern-parlour or on board a greenwich steamboat or in a jaunt to hampstead with mr
finucane his colleague at the pall mall gazette or in a visit to the summer theatre across the river or to the royal gardens of vauxhall where he be on term of friendship with the great simpson and where he shake the principal comic singer of the lovely equestrian of the arena by the hand
and while he could watch the grimace or the grace of these with a satiric humour that be not deprive of sympathy he could look on with an eye of kindness at the looker-on too at the roystere youth bent upon enjoyment and here take it at the honest parent with their delighted child laugh and clap their hand at the show at the poor outcast whose laughter be less innocent though perhaps louder and who bring their shame and their youth here to dance and be merry till the dawn at least and to get bread and drown care
of this sympathy with all condition of man arthur often boast say he be pleased to possess it and that he hop thus to the last he should retain it
as another man have an ardour for art or music or natural science mr
pen say that anthropology be his favourite pursuit and have his eye always eagerly open to its infinite variety and beauty contemplate with an unfailing delight all specimen of it in all place to which he resort whether it be the coquetting of a wrinkle dowager in a ballroom or a high-breed young beauty blushing in her prime there whether it be a hulking guardsman coax a servant-girl in the park--or innocent little tommy that be feed the duck whilst the nurse listen
and indeed a man whose heart be pretty clean can indulge in this pursuit with an enjoyment that never cease and be only perhaps the more keen because it be secret and have a touch of sadness in it because he be of his mood and humour lonely and apart although not alone

chapter l or near the temple garden fashion have long desert the green and pretty temple garden which in shakespeare make york and lancaster to pluck the innocent white and red rose which become the badge of their bloody war and the learn and pleasant writer of the handbook of london tell we that the commonest and hardy kind of rose have long cease to put forth a bud in that smoky air
not many of the present occupier of the building round about the quarter know or care very likely whether or not rose grow there or pass the old gate except on their way to chamber

chapter li the happy village again early in this history we have have occasion to speak of the little town of clavering near which pen s paternal home of fairoaks stand and of some of the people who inhabite the place and as the society there be by no mean amusing or pleasant our report concern it be not carry to any very great length

and as that duty be perform quite noiselessly while the supplication which endow she with the requisite strength for fulfil it also take place in her own chamber away from all mortal sight we too must be perforce silent about these virtue of her which no more bear public talk about than a flower will bear to bloom in a ballroom
this only we will say--that a good woman be the lovely flower that bloom under heaven and that we look with love and wonder upon its silent grace its pure fragrance its delicate bloom of beauty
sweet and beautiful the fairest and the most spotless is it not pity to see they bow down or devour by grief or death inexorable--waste in disease--pin with long pain--or cut off by sudden fate in their prime
we may deserve grief--but why should these be unhappy except that we know that heaven chasten those whom it love well be please by repeat trial to make these pure spirit more pure

let the reader who have never have a fever in chamber pity the wretch who be bind to undergo that calamity
a committee of marriageable lady or of any christian person interested in the propagation of the domestic virtue should employ a cruikshank or a leech or some other kindly expositor of the folly of the day to make a series of design represent the horror of a bachelor s life in chamber and lead the beholder to think of well thing and a more wholesome condition
what can be more uncomfortable than the bachelor s lonely breakfast with the black kettle in the dreary fire in midsummer or bad still with the fire go out at christmas half an hour after the laundress have quit the sitting-room

flanagan who be absent without leave
or again what can form a fine subject for the classical designer than the bachelor s shirt--that garment which he want to assume just at dinner-time and which he find without any button to fasten it
then there be the bachelor s return to chamber after a merry christmas holiday spend in a cosy country-house full of pretty face and kind welcome and regret
he leave his portmanteau at the barber s in the court he light his dismal old candle at the sputtering little lamp on the stair he enter the blank familiar room where the only token to greet he that show any interest in his personal welfare be the christmas bill which be lie in wait for he amiably spread out on his reading-table
add to these scene an appalling picture of bachelor s illness and the rent in the temple will begin to fall from the day of the publication of the dismal diorama
to be well in chamber be melancholy and lonely and selfish enough but to be ill in chamber--to pass long night of pain and watchfulness--to long for the morning and the laundress--to serve yourself your own medicine by your own watch--to have no other companion for long hour but your own sickening fancy and fevere thought no kind hand to give you drink if you be thirsty or to smooth the hot pillow that crumple under you this indeed be a fate so dismal and tragic that we shall not enlarge upon its horror and shall only heartily pity those bachelor in the temple who brave it every day
this lot befell arthur pendennis after the various excess which we have mention and to which he have subject his unfortunate brain

huxter in write home to his clavering friend mention that there be a fashionable club in london of which he be an attendant and that he be there in the habit of meet an irish officer of distinction who amongst other news have give that intelligence regard pendennis which the young surgeon have transmit to clavering
this club be no other than the back kitchen where the disciple of saint bartholomew be accustom to meet the general the peculiarity of whose brogue appearance disposition and general conversation greatly divert many young gentleman who use the back kitchen as a place of nightly entertainment and refreshment
huxter who have a fine natural genius for mimic everything whether it be a favourite tragic or comic actor or a cock on a dunghill a corkscrew go into a bottle and a cork issue thence or an irish officer of genteel connexion who offer himself as an object of imitation with only too much readiness talk his talk and twang his poor old long bow whenever drink a hearer and an opportunity occur study our friend the general with peculiar gusto and draw the honest fellow out many a night
a bait consist of sixpennyworth of brandy-and-water the worthy old man be sure to swallow and under the influence of this liquor who be more happy than he to tell his story of his daughter s triumph and his own in love war drink and polite society
thus huxter be enable to present to his friend many picture of costigan of costigan fight a jewel in the phaynix--of costigan and his interview with the juke of york--of costigan at his sonunlaw s teeble surround by the nobilitee of his countree--of costigan when cry drunk at which time he be in the habit of confidentially lament his daughter s ingratichewd and state that his grey hair be hasten to a praymachure greeve
and thus our friend be the mean of bring a number of young fellow to the back kitchen who consume the landlord s liquor whilst they relish the general s peculiarity so that mine host pardon many of the latter s foible in consideration of the good which they bring to his house
not the high position in life be this--certainly or one which if we have a reverence for an old man we would be anxious that he should occupy but of this aged buffoon it may be mention that he have no particular idea that his condition of life be not a high one and that in his whiskie blood there be not a black drop nor in his muddled brain a bitter feeling against any mortal being
even his child his cruel emily he would have take to his heart and forgive with tear and what more can one say of the christian charity of a man than that he be actually ready to forgive those who have do he every kindness and with whom he be wrong in a dispute
there be some idea amongst the young man who frequent the back kitchen and make themselves merry with the society of captain costigan that the captain make a mystery regard his lodging for fear of dun or from a desire of privacy and live in some wonderful place
nor would the landlord of the premise when question upon this subject answer any inquiry his maxim be that he only know gentleman who frequent that room in that room that when they quit that room have pay their score as gentleman and behave as gentleman his communication with they cease and that as a gentleman himself he think it be only impertinent curiosity to ask where any other gentleman live
costigan in his most intoxicated and confidential moment also evade any reply to question or hint address to he on this subject there be no particular secret about it as we have see who have have more than once the honour of enter his apartment but in the vicissitude of a long life he have be pretty often in the habit of reside in house where privacy be necessary to his comfort and where the appearance of some visitor would have bring he anything but pleasure
hence all sort of legend be form by wag or credulous person respect his place of abode
it be state that he sleep habitually in a watch-box in the city in a cab at a mews where a cab-proprietor give he a shelter in the duke of york s column etc the wild of these theory be put abroad by the facetious and imaginative huxter
for huxey when not silence by the company of swell and when in the society of his own friend be a very different fellow to the youth whom we have see cow by pen s impertinent air and adore by his family at home be the life and soul of the circle whom he meet either round the festive board or the dissect table
on one brilliant september morning as huxter be regale himself with a cup of coffee at a stall in covent garden have spend a delicious night dance at vauxhall he spy the general reel down henrietta street with a crowd of hoot blackguard boy at his heel who have leave their bed under the arch of the river betime and be prowl about already for breakfast and the strange livelihood of the day
the poor old general be not in that condition when the sneer and joke of these young beggar have much effect upon he the cabman and waterman at the cabstand know he and pass their comment upon he the policeman gaze after he and warn the boy off he with look of scorn and pity what do the scorn and pity of man the joke of ribald child matter to the general
he reel along the street with glaze eye have just sense enough to know whither he be bind and to pursue his accustomed beat homeward

here just as he be within sight of home a luckless slice of orange-peel come between the general s heel and the pavement and cause the poor old fellow to fall backwards
huxter run up to he instantly and after a pause during which the veteran giddy with his fall and his previous whisky gather as he well might his dizzy brain together the young surgeon lift up the limping general and very kindly and good-naturedly offer to conduct he to his home

and the tete-a-tete of the two gentleman be break up by the entry of other gentleman member of the club--when fashionable town-talk politic cribbage and other amusement ensue and the conversation become general
the gentleman s club be hold in the parlour of the wheel of fortune public-house in a snug little by-lane lead out of one of the great street of mayfair and frequent by some of the most select gentleman about town
their master affair debt intrigue adventure their lady good and bad quality and quarrel with their husband all the family secret be here discuss with perfect freedom and confidence and here when about to enter into a new situation a gentleman be enable to get every requisite information regard the family of which he propose to become a member
livery it may be imagine be exclude from this select precinct and the powdered head of the large metropolitan footman might bow down in vain entreating admission into the gentleman s club
these outcast giant in plush take their beer in an outer apartment of the wheel of fortune and could no more get an entry into the clubroom than a pall mall tradesman or a lincoln s inn attorney could get admission into bays s or spratt s
and it be because the conversation which we have permit to overhear here in some measure explain the character and bearing of our story that we have venture to introduce the reader into a society so exclusive
chapter lxii the way of the world a short time after the piece of good fortune which befell colonel altamont at epsom that gentleman put into execution his project foreign tour and the chronicler of the polite world who go down to london bridge for the purpose of take leave of the people of fashion who quit this country announce that among the company on board the soho to antwerp last saturday be sir robert lady and the misses hodge mr
serjeant kewsy and mrs
and miss kewsy colonel altamont major coddy etc the colonel travel in state and as become a gentleman he appear in a rich travel costume he drink brandy-and-water freely during the passage and be not sick as some of the other passenger be and he be attend by his body-servant the faithful irish legionary who have be for some time in wait upon himself and captain strong in their chamber of shepherd s inn

strong s chief business

it be rather a grand and touching sight now to see the portly and handsome gentleman paint his own boot and broil his own mutton chop

iwill give you half ned upon my soul and honour iwill give you half if you can get anybody to do we a little fifty but ned say sternly that he have give his word of honour as a gentleman that he would be no party to any future bill transaction in which her husband might engage who have give his word of honour too and the chevalier say that he at least would keep his word and would black his own boot all his life rather than break his promise

as you sit surround by respect and affection happy honour and flatter in your old age your foible gently indulge your least word kindly cherish your garrulous old story receive for the hundredth time with dutiful forbearance and never-fail hypocritical smile the woman of your house constant in their flattery the young man hush and attentive when you begin to speak the servant awestricken the tenant cap in hand and ready to act in the place of your worship s horse when your honour take a drive--it have often strike you o thoughtful dive

it seem to i that my scepticism be more respectful and more modest than the revolutionary ardour of other folk

a college tutor or a nobleman s toady who appear one fine day as my right reverend lord in a silk apron and a shovel-hat and assume benedictory air over i be still the same man we remember at oxbridge when he be truckle to the tuft and bully the poor undergraduate in the lecture-room
an hereditary legislator who pass his time with jockey and black-leg and ballet-girl and who be call to rule over i and his other better because his grandfather make a lucky speculation in the fund or find a coal or tin mine on his property or because his stupid ancestor happen to be in command of ten thousand man as brave as himself who overcome twelve thousand frenchmen or fifty thousand indians--such a man i say inspire i with no more respect than the bitter democrat can feel towards he
but such as he be he be a part of the old society to which we belong and i submit to his lordship with acquiescence and he take his place above the good of we at all dinner-party and there bid his time
i donot want to chop his head off with a guillotine or to fle mud at he in the street

what i argue here be that i will not persecute

but if the begum pay clavering s debt many time more her wealth would be expend altogether upon this irreclaimable reprobate and her heir whoever they might be would succeed but to an emptied treasury and miss amory instead of bring her husband a good income and a seat in parliament would bring to that individual her person only and her pedigree with that lamentable note of sus
per coll
at the name of the last male of her line
there be however to the old schemer revolve these thing in his mind another course yet open the which will appear to the reader who may take the trouble to peruse a conversation which presently ensue between major pendennis and the honourable baronet the member for clavering
when a man under pecuniary difficulty disappear from among his usual friend and equals dive out of sight as it be from the flock of bird in which he be accustomed to sail it be wonderful at what strange and distant nook he come up again for breath
i have know a pall mall lounger and rotten row buck of no inconsiderable fashion vanish from amongst his comrade of the clubs and the park and be discover very happy and affable at an eighteenpenny ordinary in billingsgate another gentleman of great learning and wit when outrun the constable be i to say he be a literary man some critic would vow that i intend to insult the literary profession once send i his address at a little public-house call the fox under the hill down a most darksome and cavernous archway in the strand
such a man under such misfortune may have a house but he be never in his house and have an address where letter may be leave but only simpleton go with the hope of see him only a few of the faithful know where he be to be find and have the clue to his hiding-place
so after the dispute with his wife and the misfortune consequent thereon to find sir francis clavering at home be impossible
ever since i hast he for my book which be fourteen pound he donot come home till three o clock and purtend to be asleep when i bring his water of a mornin and dodge hout when ibe downstairs mr
lightfoot remark to his friend morgan and announce that he should go down to my lady and be butler there and marry his old woman
in like manner after his altercation with strong the baronet do not come near he and flee to other haunt out of the reach of the chevalier s reproaches out of the reach of conscience if possible which many of we try to dodge and leave behind we by change of scene and other fugitive stratagem
so though the elder pendennis have his own ulterior object be bent upon see pen s country neighbour and representative in parliament it take the major no inconsiderable trouble and time before he could get he into such a confidential state and conversation as be necessary for the end which the major have in view
for since the major have be call in as family friend and have cognisance of clavering s affair conjugal and pecuniary the baronet avoid he as he always avoid all his lawyer and agent when there be an account to be render or an affair of business to be discuss between they and never keep any appointment but when its object be the raising of money
thus previous to catch this most shy and timorous bird the major make more than one futile attempt to hold him on one day it be a most innocent-look invitation to dinner at greenwich to meet a few friend the baronet accept suspect something and do not come leave the major who indeed propose to represent in himself the body of friend to eat his whitebait alone on another occasion the major write and ask for ten minute talk and the baronet instantly acknowledge the note and make the appointment at four o clock the next day at bays s precisely he carefully underline the precisely but though four o clock come as in the course of time and destiny it could not do otherwise no clavering make his appearance

chapter lxiv phyllis and corydon on a picturesque common in the neighbourhood of tunbridge wells lady clavering have find a pretty villa whither she retire after her conjugal dispute at the end of that unlucky london season
miss amory of course accompany her mother and master clavering come home for the holiday with whom blanche s chief occupation be to fight and quarrel
but this be only a home pastime and the young schoolboy be not fond of home sport
he find cricket and horse and plenty of friend at tunbridge
the good-natured begum s house be fill with a constant society of young gentleman of thirteen who eat and drink much too copiously of tart and champagne who ride race on the lawn and frighten the fond mother who smoke and make themselves sick and the dining-room unbearable to miss blanche
she do not like the society of young gentleman of thirteen
as for that fair young creature any change as long as it be change be pleasant to she and for a week or two she would have like poverty and a cottage and bread-and-cheese and for a night perhaps a dungeon and bread-and-water and so the move to tunbridge be by no mean unwelcome to she
she wander in the wood and sketch tree and farmhouse she read french novel habitually she drive into tunbridge wells pretty often and to any play or ball or conjurer or musician who might happen to appear in the place she sleep a great deal she quarrel with mamma and frank during the morning she find the little village school and attend it and first fondle the girl and thwart the mistress then scold the girl and laugh at the teacher she be constant at church of course
it be a pretty little church of immense antiquity--a little anglo-norman bijou build the day before yesterday and decorate with all sort of paint window carve saint head gilt scripture text and open pew
blanche begin forthwith to work a most correct high-church altar-cover for the church
she pass for a saint with the clergyman for a while whom she quite take in and whom she coax and wheedle and fondle so artfully that poor mrs
smirke who at first be charm with she then bear with she then would hardly speak to she be almost mad with jealousy
mrs
smirke be the wife of our old friend smirke pen s tutor and poor helen s suitor
he have console himself for her refusal with a young lady from clapham whom his mamma provide
when the latter die our friend s view become every day more and more pronounced
he cut off his coat collar and let his hair grow over his back
he rigorously give up the curl which he use to sport on his forehead and the tie of his neckcloth of which he be rather proud
he go without any tie at all
he go without dinner on fridays
he read the roman hour and intimate that he be ready to receive confession in the vestry
the most harmless creature in the world he be denounce as a black and most dangerous jesuit and papist by muffin of the dissenting chapel and mr
simeon knight at the old church
mr
smirke have build his chapel-of-ease with the money leave he by his mother at clapham
lord
lord
what would she have say to hear a table call an altar
to see candlestick on it

colonel altamont have not be for many week absent on his foreign tour sir francis clavering have retire meanwhile into the country pursuant to his agreement with major pendennis when the ill of fate begin to fall rather suddenly and heavily upon the sole remain partner of the little firm of shepherd s inn
when strong at part with altamont refuse the loan proffer by the latter in the fulness of his purse and the generosity of his heart he make such a sacrifice to conscience and delicacy as cause he many an after twinge and pang he and feel--it be not very many hour in his life he have experience the feeling--that in this juncture of his affair he have be too delicate and too scrupulous
why should a fellow in want refuse a kind offer kindly make

it be whisper among the tradesman bill-discounter and other who have commercial dealing with captain strong that he and the baronet have part company and that the captain s paper be henceforth of no value
the tradesman who have put a wonderful confidence in he hitherto for who could resist strong s jolly face and frank and honest demeanour now begin to pour in their bill with a cowardly mistrust and unanimity
the knock at the shepherd s inn chamber door be constant and tailor bootmaker pastrycook who have furnish dinner in their own person or by the boy their representative hold levee on strong s stair

etc
from this invasion of creditor poor strong who have not a guinea in his pocket have of course no refuge but that of the englishman s castle into which he retire shut the outer and inner door upon the enemy and not quit his stronghold until after nightfall
against this outer barrier the foe use to come and knock and curse in vain whilst the chevalier peep at they from behind the little curtain which he have put over the orifice of his letter-box and have the dismal satisfaction of see the face of furious clerk and fiery dun as they dash up against the door and retreat from it
but as they could not be always at his gate or sleep on his staircase the enemy of the chevalier sometimes leave he free
strong when so pressed by his commercial antagonist be not quite alone in his defence against they but have secure for himself an ally or two
his friend be instruct to communicate with he by a system of private signal and they thus keep the garrison from starve by bring in necessary supply and keep up strong s heart and prevent he from surrender by visit he and cheer he in his retreat
two of ned s most faithful ally be huxter and miss fanny bolton when hostile visitor be prowl about the inn fanny s little sister be teach a particular cry or jodel which they innocently whoop in the court when fanny and huxter come up to visit strong they archly sing this same note at his door when that barrier be straightway open the honest garrison come out smile the provision and the pot of porter be bring in and in the society of his faithful friend the beleaguered one pass a comfortable night
there be some man who could not live under this excitement but strong be a brave man as we have say who have see service and never lose heart in peril

chapter lxvii in which pen begin to doubt about his election whilst pen in his own county be thus carry on his selfish plan and parliamentary scheme news come to he that lady rockminster have arrive at baymouth and have bring with she our friend laura

arthur attire in a new hat a new blue frock-coat and blue handkerchief in a new fancy waistcoat new boot and new shirt-stud present by the right honourable the countess dowager of rockminster make his appearance at a solitary breakfast-table in clavering park where he could scarce eat a single morsel of food

pendennis whom i hope to know even long than i have already know she
and when i call her attention to the fact that one of the most necessary article to her husband s comfort be pure sherry i know i shall have she for a customer for your worship s sake
but i have to speak to you of other than my own concern

the rascal marry at sixteen and have repeatedly since perform that ceremony--in sydney in new zealand in south america in newcastle he say first before he know our poor friend the milliner
he be a perfect don juan
and it seem as if the commendatore have at last overtaken he for as we be at our meal there come three heavy knock at my outer door which make our friend start

pen s work have procure he more reputation than have be acquire by his abler friend whom no one know george life content without the fame
if the good man do not draw the great prize in life we know it have be so settle by the ordainer of the lottery
we own and see daily how the false and worthless live and prosper while the good be call away and the dear and young perish untimely we perceive in every man s life the maim happiness the frequent fall the bootless endeavour the struggle of right and wrong in which the strong often succumb and the swift fail we see flower of good blooming in foul place as in the most lofty and splendid fortune flaw of vice and meanness and stain of evil and know how mean the good of we be let we give a hand of charity to arthur pendennis with all his fault and shortcoming who do not claim to be a hero but only a man and a brother
